_meta.note: S204 causal spine: 53 S203 harvest seeds + marquee Dance chains + 38 zero-edge stubs. Proposers Sonnet x10, orchestrator-adjudicated, Haiku fresh-verified.

edges.quote: In the Bite, the lords of the Three Sisters had taken advantage of the chaos of Aegon’s Conquest to declare themselves a free nation and crown Lady Marla of House Sunderland their queen.
edges.note: The disorder of the Conquest is the precondition the Sistermen exploit to declare independence; it does not force their revolt.
edges.rationale: ENABLES over CAUSES: the Three Sisters lords make a free decision to rebel using the Conquest's chaos as opportunity, not as a forcing cause (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: House Hoare had ruled the ironmen for long centuries, only to be extinguished in a single night when Aegon unleashed Balerion’s fires on Harrenhal. Though Harren the Black and his sons perished in those flames, Qhorin Volmark of Harlaw, whose grandmother had been a younger sister of Harren’s grandsire, declared himself the rightful heir “of the black line,” and
edges.note: Harren's death in the burning of Harrenhal opens the succession Qhorin Volmark claims.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form) rather than CAUSES: Volmark's claim of the black line is his own decision seizing on the vacancy left by Harren's death, a human choice mediating the link (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: Aegon Targaryen put an end to the fighting. He descended on the islands in 2 AC, riding Balerion.
edges.note: Volmark's rival kingship (and the fratricidal war it touched off among ironborn claimants) is what draws Aegon's invasion in 2 AC.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES/TRIGGERS: Aegon's decision to personally intervene is a free royal decision responding to the ironborn succession crisis, not a mechanical consequence of it.

edges.quote: With the submission of the Three Sisters and the Iron Islands, all of Westeros south of the Wall was now ruled by Aegon Targaryen, save Dorne alone. So it was to Dorne that the Dragon next turned his attention.
edges.note: Pacifying the Iron Islands frees Aegon's attention and forces to finally turn on Dorne.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the Iron Islands' submission is a precondition (Aegon now has nothing else to subdue) but Aegon still freely chooses to invade Dorne rather than being forced to.

edges.quote: With the submission of the Three Sisters and the Iron Islands, all of Westeros south of the Wall was now ruled by Aegon Targaryen, save Dorne alone. So it was to Dorne that the Dragon next turned his attention.
edges.note: The text names both the Three Sisters' and the Iron Islands' submission as the joint precondition for Aegon turning to Dorne.
edges.rationale: ENABLES for the same reason as A-E04; the Sistermen's submission is the other named half of the precondition, not a forcing cause on its own.

edges.quote: Riding Meraxes, the queen descended out of a clear blue sky and set the Planky Town ablaze, the fires leaping from boat to boat until the whole mouth of the Greenblood was choked with burning flotsam, and the pillar of smoke could be seen as far away as Sunspear.
edges.note: Rhaenys's burning of the Planky Town is explicitly the opening act ("first blood") of the First Dornish War.
edges.rationale: Structural PART_OF: it is the war's opening battle, not a separate causal claim; matches the existing PART_OF pattern already on Defenestration of Sunspear.

edges.quote: When word of the Defenestration of Sunspear reached the Hellholt, Lord Tyrell gathered his remaining strength and set off across the sands. His announced intention was to capture Vaith, march east along the river, retake Sunspear and the shadow city, and punish Lord Rosby’s murderers. But somewhere east of the Hellholt amidst the red sands, Tyrell and his entire army disappeared.
edges.note: News of Rosby's murder at Sunspear is the explicit reason Tyrell marches out from the Hellholt — the march on which he vanishes.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form): Tyrell's march to avenge Rosby is his own decision; the defenestration does not mechanically force his disappearance, it drives the choice that leads to it.

edges.quote: In 7 AC, Orys Baratheon and the other lords who had been taken captive on the Boneway were ransomed back to King's Landing for their weight in gold, but on their return it was found that the Widow-lover had lopped off each man’s sword hand, so they might never again take up swords against Dorne. In retaliation, King Aegon himself descended on the mountain fastnesses of the Wyls with Balerion, and reduced half a dozen of their keeps and watchtowers to heaps of molten stone.
edges.note: Seed row 23: the Widow-lover's maiming of the ransomed captives draws Aegon's direct Balerion reprisal on the Wyl mountain keeps.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES over TRIGGERS: the text's own ‘In retaliation’ reads as immediate, but Aegon's decision to personally fly to war is still a human choice mediating the link (agency-collapse check), so it is routed as a decision-event target rather than collapsed into CAUSES/TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: In 7 AC, Orys Baratheon and the other lords who had been taken captive on the Boneway were ransomed back to King's Landing for their weight in gold, but on their return it was found that the Widow-lover had lopped off each man’s sword hand, so they might never again take up swords against Dorne.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: a marquee sub-beat of the named war, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: In retaliation, King Aegon himself descended on the mountain fastnesses of the Wyls with Balerion, and reduced half a dozen of their keeps and watchtowers to heaps of molten stone.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: a marquee sub-beat of the named war, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: Lord Orys was taken captive during the Dornish War and suffered the loss of his sword hand. When ransomed back, his lordship asked the king to be relieved of his duties. “The King’s Hand should have a hand,” he said.
edges.note: The maiming is the explicit, stated reason Orys resigns as Hand of the King.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition/character-target form): his own mutilation drives Orys's personal decision to resign, not a mechanical consequence.

edges.quote: But as Meraxes banked above the Hellholt, a defender atop the castle's highest tower triggered a scorpion, and a yard-long iron bolt caught the queen's dragon in the right eye. Meraxes did not die at once, but came crashing to earth in mortal agony
edges.note: Seed row 24: the scorpion bolt fells Meraxes, and the dragon's crash is the immediate mechanism of Rhaenys's death. [S204 adjudication: TRIGGERS->CAUSES tier-1 — death-from-downing undisputed; MECHANISM disputed (fall vs later torture), noted not tiered]
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: no decision intervenes between dragon and rider — Meraxes's fatal fall is the direct physical mechanism of Rhaenys's death (whichever of the disputed accounts of her exact end is true), the tightest link on the ladder.

edges.quote: But as Meraxes banked above the Hellholt, a defender atop the castle's highest tower triggered a scorpion, and a yard-long iron bolt caught the queen's dragon in the right eye.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: the war's single greatest Targaryen loss, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: Rhaenys Targaryen, sister and wife to King Aegon |, perished at the Hellholt in Dorne in the 10th year After the Conquest.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: a marquee sub-beat of the named war, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: The next two years were the years of the Dragon’s Wroth. Every castle in Dorne, save Sunspear, was burned thrice over, as Balerion and Vhagar returned time and time again.
edges.note: Seed row 24: Rhaenys's death is what the two-year reprisal campaign is named and dated from ("the next two years" after her 10 AC death).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Aegon and Visenya's grief-driven decision to burn Dorne repeatedly is a human/royal choice responding to the death, not a mechanical CAUSES; the campaign is itself a sustained decision-event.

edges.quote: The next two years were the years of the Dragon’s Wroth. Every castle in Dorne, save Sunspear, was burned thrice over, as Balerion and Vhagar returned time and time again.
edges.note: Structural containment for the new mint.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: the war's climactic reprisal phase, explicitly named within the war's own narrative, not an umbrella parent.

edges.quote: Aegon was the Lord of the Seven Kingdoms; therefore, he should have seven champions, Queen Visenya decided. Thus did the Kingsguard come into being;
edges.note: The Dornish counter-assassination attempts on Aegon and Visenya during the Dragon's Wroth are what push Visenya to institute the seven-strong Kingsguard (governance section, lines 279-283, same unit). [S204 adjudication: re-quoted to the founding-decision sentence per fresh-verify]
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Visenya's decision to found the Kingsguard is her own free choice provoked by the repeated attacks, not a forced consequence of them.

edges.quote: The most infamous act of that bloody age occurred in 12 AC, when Wyl of Wyl, the Widow-lover, arrived uninvited at the wedding of Ser Jon Cafferen, heir to Fawnton, to Alys Oakheart, daughter to the Lord of Old Oak.
edges.note: The text explicitly frames the wedding massacre as the single most infamous act of the Dragon's Wroth period ("that bloody age").
edges.rationale: SUB_BEAT_OF: the wedding raid is a discrete atrocity inside the named reprisal-era hub, giving the previously causally-dark roster node its first structural edge.

edges.quote: By then Dorne was a smoking desert, beset by famine, plague, and blight. “A blasted land,” traders from the Free Cities called it.
edges.note: The devastation of the Dragon's Wroth is what leaves Dorne, per Grand Maester Clegg later in the same passage, without the strength to keep fighting, making Nymor's peace overture possible. [S204 adjudication: tier-2 — causal link rests on Grand Maester Clegg's attributed analysis ('Dorne no longer had the strength to fight')]
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the ruin of Dorne is a precondition for peace, not a forcing cause — Nymor still freely chooses to sue for peace rather than fight on (House Martell ‘still remained Unbowed, Unbent, Unbroken’ in the same line).

edges.quote: King Aegon read Prince Nymor's words in open court, stone-faced and silent, whilst seated on the Iron Throne. When he rose afterward, men said, his hand was dripping blood. He burned the letter and never spoke of it again, but that night he mounted Balerion and flew off across the waters of Blackwater Bay, to Dragonstone upon its smoking mountain.
edges.note: Section note: the mysterious letter is the proximate trigger that makes Aegon, on the point of refusing peace, relent overnight and agree to Nymor's terms the next morning.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Aegon's overnight change of heart and subsequent assent to peace is his own sovereign decision provoked by the letter's unknown contents, not a mechanical CAUSES — the text is explicit that the king was 'on the point of refusing' until the letter intervened.

edges.quote: This bequest would prove to be most unwise, as later events would demonstrate.
edges.note: Aenys's coronation gift of Blackfyre and 'we shall rule together' pledge to Maegor
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: Gyldayn explicitly flags the bequest as feeding Maegor's later claim to sole rule, but Maegor's decision to act on it comes years later and is his own free choice (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: Within an hour of the king's death, she had mounted Vhagar and flown east across the narrow sea. When she returned, Prince Maegor was with her, on Balerion.
edges.note: Visenya fetches exiled Maegor from Pentos within an hour of Aenys's death
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the very next beat after Aenys's death, no decisional gap — Visenya acts within the hour.

edges.quote: “The Iron Throne will go to the man who has the strength to seize it," Maegor replied. Whereupon he decreed the immediate execution of the Grand Maester, taking off Gawen’s old grey head himself with a single swing of Blackfyre.
edges.note: Gawen's objection to Maegor's self-coronation gets him beheaded on the spot
edges.rationale: CAUSES: Maegor's own decree of execution is a direct real-causation response to Gawen's objection to the just-claimed throne, narrated as one continuous beat.

edges.quote: Prince Aegon was determined to claim his birthright.
edges.note: Being supplanted by his uncle drives Aegon's later rebellion
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: Aegon's rebellion is his own free decision to contest the usurpation, not an automatic consequence.

edges.quote: A hush fell over the hall at those words, for all present knew that title had hitherto belonged to Prince Maegor.
edges.note: Stripping Maegor of Prince of Dragonstone in favor of Aegon feeds Maegor's grievance
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: this public humiliation is cited by Gyldayn as souring the brothers' relationship and feeding Maegor's later 'strength to seize it' claim, mediated by Maegor's own choice years later.

edges.quote: At the feast afterward, King Aenys compounded his misjudgment by granting the title Prince of Dragonstone to his presumptive heir, Prince Aegon.
edges.note: At the wedding feast itself, Aenys grants Aegon the title that had belonged to Maegor
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'at the feast afterward' — the very next beat of the same occasion, no decisional gap.

edges.quote: The Faithful were listening. Less than a fortnight later, as Septon Murmison was crossing the city in his litter, a group of Poor Fellows came swarming from an alley and hacked him to pieces with their axes.
edges.note: Poor Fellows hack Murmison to pieces after he officiated the forbidden brother-sister wedding
edges.rationale: CAUSES: several intermediate steps (Murmison performing the rite, his expulsion from the Faith, the High Septon's 'King Abomination' denunciation) sit between the marriage and the killing, so CAUSES rather than TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: Three days before they were to sail, two Poor Fellows scaled the manse’s walls and broke into the king's bedchamber. Only the timely intervention of the Kingsguard saved Aenys from an ignoble death.
edges.note: Murmison's murder is followed directly by an assassination attempt on Aenys himself
edges.rationale: CAUSES: same wave of Faith Militant radicalization that killed Murmison escalates immediately to an attempt on the king's own life; the Kingsguard's intervention (a third party's action) is what prevents the outcome, so CAUSES (not TRIGGERS, since the manse fortification/relocation decision intervenes).

edges.quote: or suffer five years of exile. Prince Maegor chose exile.
edges.note: Aenys's ultimatum over the Alys Harroway marriage forces Maegor's choice of exile
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: Maegor's choice of exile over abandoning Alys is his own free decision (agency-collapse check) made in response to the marriage scandal.

edges.quote: But the revels died away when Maegor mounted Balerion and descended upon the Hill of Rhaenys, where seven hundred of the Warrior's Sons were at their morning prayers in the fortified sept.
edges.note: Recovered from his Trial of Seven wounds, Maegor burns the surviving Warrior's Sons at their fortified sept
edges.rationale: CAUSES: Maegor's dragonfire attack is the direct sequel to his survival of the trial (28 days of recovery under Tyanna's care sit between), a real multi-step causal chain rather than an instantaneous trigger.

edges.quote: Battle was joined first in the Reach, at the town of Stonebridge.
edges.note: The High Septon's call to arms after the sept-burning produces open battle at Stonebridge
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: the High Septon's decision to call 'true and pious children of the gods' to arms is a free decision by a third party mediating between the burning and the battle (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: King Maegor let the poisons fester for almost half a year, so engrossed was he in the building of his Red Keep. It was his mother who struck first.
edges.note: Victory at the Great Fork frees the crown's hand, but Maegor delays half a year before the burnings begin
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the military defeat of the Faith's field army is a precondition for the punitive burning campaign but does not force its timing or occurrence — Visenya's own decision ('it was his mother who struck first') completes the causation.

edges.quote: His Grace's long absence from King’s Landing had not gone unnoticed
edges.note: Maegor's extended stay in Oldtown after the Faith's submission leaves King's Landing unguarded
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Maegor's prolonged absence is an opportunity, not a compulsion — Aegon's own decision to seize the moment completes the causation, preserving agency.

edges.quote: Emerging at last from Casterly Rock, Aegon the Uncrowned and his wife, Rhaena, raced across the riverlands with a handful of companions and entered the city concealed beneath sacks of corn.
edges.note: The dragon-theft raid is the opening beat of Aegon's rebellion against Maegor
edges.rationale: Structural PART_OF: the raid to reclaim Quicksilver and Dreamfyre is narrated as the direct opening move of the wider rebellion that culminates at the Battle Beneath the Gods Eye.

edges.quote: Lady Jeyne of House Westerling had been married to Alyn Tarbeck, who had died with Prince Aegon in the Battle Beneath the Gods Eye.
edges.note: The battle's dead (Aegon, Alyn Tarbeck) create the pool of 'widows of proven fertility' Maegor marries three years later
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the battle's casualties are the precondition that makes Rhaena and Jeyne available as brides, but Maegor's decision to marry three widows at once is his own free choice.

edges.quote: Dragged from her bed, Queen Alys saw
edges.note: The monstrous stillbirth triggers Maegor's investigation, torture, and confessions that end in Alys's arrest and death
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the stillbirth sets off a direct, textually explicit chain (suspicion → Tyanna's list → torture → confessions → arrest) ending in Alys's execution; real causation through intermediate steps.

edges.quote: King Maegor himself departed King’s Landing, assembling a strong force of knights and men-at-arms and marching on Harrenhal to complete the destruction of House Harroway.
edges.note: Maegor personally leads the campaign that annihilates House Harroway immediately after Alys's execution
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the text frames the military campaign as a direct continuation ('to complete the destruction') of the same purge that killed Alys.

edges.quote: On the nights when the king was sharing a bed with Queen Ceryse or Queen Tyanna, Lucas Harroway sent men to his daughter's bed to get her with child.
edges.note: Lucas Harroway's standing as Hand of the King gives him the access and cover to arrange the scheme that produces the stillbirth
edges.rationale: ENABLES: his position as Hand is a precondition of opportunity, not a guarantee — the scheme itself is Lucas and Alys's own decision (stub wired).

edges.quote: Seeing Queen Ceryse return to court, old and bitter and childless, Alys Harroway had begun to fear that the same fate awaited her unless. she gave the king a son, so she had turned to her lord father, the Hand of the King.
edges.note: Ceryse's restoration to court (from the Oldtown reconciliation) drives Alys's fear-based decision to seek an heir by illicit means
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Ceryse's return is the motivating fear behind Alys's own decision to conspire with her father, a human decision mediates so MOTIVATES not CAUSES.

edges.quote: In the aftermath of the wedding, Maegor declared Rhaena’s daughter Aerea his lawful heir
edges.note: Maegor names Rhaena's daughter Aerea his heir in the immediate aftermath of the triple wedding
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'in the aftermath of the wedding' — the decree is the very next beat of the same occasion.

edges.quote: His nephew Jaehaerys, the rightful heir by all the laws of the Seven Kingdoms, was expressly disinherited in the same decree.
edges.note: Jaehaerys's disinheritance is a core grievance behind Lord Rogar's later proclamation of him as true king
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the uprising is a deliberate political act by Rogar Baratheon and Jaehaerys, not an automatic consequence of the decree.

edges.quote: But even in death, Tyanna of the Tower had her revenge, for it came to pass just as she had promised.
edges.note: Tyanna's promised poisoning of Elinor's child comes to pass after Maegor kills her
edges.rationale: CAUSES, but disputed: Gyldayn frames this as revenge 'even in death,' an inherently uncertain/folkloric causal claim (poison administered earlier vs. coincidence); no living decision mediates the stillbirth itself, so CAUSES rather than MOTIVATES, but tier-2 + disputed given the supernatural framing.

edges.quote: No man in the Seven Kingdoms could doubt that the king was accursed now. What followers still remained to him began to melt away, evaporating like dew in the morning sun.
edges.note: The third monstrous stillbirth convinces lords Maegor is doomed, and they begin abandoning him for Jaehaerys
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the perception that the king is 'accursed' is the psychological opening the defecting lords use to justify rebellion, a human political decision mediates.

edges.quote: When word reached the Red Keep that Jaehaerys was gathering his forces at Storm's End, Rhaena Targaryen mounted Dreamfyre and flew to join him, abandoning the uncle she had been forced to wed.
edges.note: Rhaena flies to join Jaehaerys the moment word of his muster at Storm's End reaches her
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: her flight is the immediate next beat upon hearing the news, no other decision intervenes (stub wired).

edges.quote: Queen Elinor found him still upon the Iron Throne, pale and dead, his robes soaked through with blood.
edges.note: Maegor is found dead on the Iron Throne the same night his war council collapses under the mass defections
edges.rationale: CAUSES, but disputed: the uprising's collapse of Maegor's support (down to four thousand men, Lord Hayford urging abdication) is the plain causal frame Gyldayn gives, but the literal mechanism of death is explicitly disputed in-text (murder by the throne's blades, Elinor, the Kingsguard, or suicide), so tier-2 + disputed.

edges.quote: In their place, he decreed, a great stone “stable for dragons” would be erected, a lair worthy of Balerion, Vhagar, and their get. Thus commenced the building of the Dragonpit.
edges.note: The ashes and ruins of the burned Sept of Remembrance are cleared to make way for the Dragonpit
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the earlier burning is the physical precondition (the ruin that gets cleared) for the Dragonpit's construction years later, but Maegor's decision to build there is his own choice.

edges.quote: In the chaos that had followed the discovery of Maegor’s body, Lord Rosby drank a cup of hemlock to join his king in death.
edges.note: Rosby kills himself in the chaos immediately following the discovery of Maegor's body
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Rosby's suicide is his own free decision made in direct response to his king's death; target is the decision-event itself (agency-preserving).

edges.quote: Moon's murder removed the last major obstacle to the accession of Jaehaerys Targaryen to the Iron Throne
edges.note: Septon Moon's assassination removes the last obstacle blocking Jaehaerys's safe journey to be crowned at Oldtown (seed row 11)
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: Gyldayn's own word is 'obstacle removed' — the coronation still requires Jaehaerys's court to travel to Oldtown and the High Septon to perform the rite, decisional steps by multiple parties.

edges.quote: After seizing the septon’s body from Rob the Starvling, this “learned” fool strapped it atop a destrier, naked, bloody, and rotting, to storm the gates of Oldtown.
edges.note: Lorcas seizes Moon's corpse and mounts a doomed attack on Oldtown in the immediate aftermath of the assassination
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: Lorcas's attack follows directly from the power vacuum and corpse-dispute created by Moon's murder, the next beat in the same collapse (stub wired).

edges.quote: it was widely believed that someone—Queen Rhaena herself, mayhaps, or her mother, Queen Alyssa—had used the occasion of the king's coronation to switch the twins.
edges.note: The gathering of the whole family at Jaehaerys's coronation is the occasion used to secretly swap Aerea and Rhaella
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the coronation creates the opportunity (both twins together, one returning to King's Landing) but does not compel the switch, which is Rhaena/Alyssa's own alleged decision; tier-2 + disputed since the text says 'nothing was ever proved.'

edges.quote: underestimated the will and determination of Alysanne Targaryen herself, and her young king, Jaehaerys.
edges.note: Council's secret betrothal of Alysanne to Orryn Baratheon drives Jaehaerys and Alysanne to flee to Dragonstone and marry in secret (seed row 312).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES/TRIGGERS: the betrothal is the provocation, but the flight and secret wedding are Jaehaerys and Alysanne's own free decision (agency-collapse check) -- target is the decision-event they produced.

edges.quote: That omission would prove to be of great importance when Lord Rogar and Queen Alyssa arrived belatedly from King's Landing in a war galley, accompanied by a dozen knights, forty men-at-arms, Septon Mattheus, and
edges.note: News of the unconsummated secret marriage draws Rogar and Alyssa to Dragonstone in force to try to undo it.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Rogar and Alyssa's voyage to intervene is their own decision made in response to the marriage, not a mechanical inevitability -- routes around agency-collapse.

edges.quote: The immediate cause of the tension was the king’s sudden and secret marriage to his sister
edges.note: Gyldayn names the secret marriage the 'immediate cause of the tension' between king and Hand that festers for a year until Rogar moves to depose Jaehaerys (seed row 93; grounding located in fab-surfeit-of-rulers-08-p01, the unit the S203 harvest row left null).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: many months and several free decisions separate the marriage from Rogar's coup attempt, but the text explicitly names the marriage as the origin of the estrangement -- a disposition edge to the decision-event where that estrangement erupts into action.

edges.quote: Alysanne must have her household, and in that her mother, Alyssa, saw an opportunity to undermine, and mayhaps undo, her marriage.
edges.note: Having failed to physically separate the couple at the gates, Alyssa (with Rogar's connivance) turns to a subtler plan: send companions to Dragonstone to undermine the marriage from within. [S204 adjudication: tier-2 — causation inferable-not-stated (failed gates confrontation -> household-undermine plan)]
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: dispatching the Wise Women is Alyssa's deliberate follow-on scheme after the failed confrontation, a free decision, not an automatic consequence.

edges.quote: “We cannot remain here,” she confided to her dear companions.
edges.note: Lyman Lannister's growing ambition for a Targaryen alliance and dragons of his own drives Rhaena to resolve to leave Casterly Rock; after further wandering this resolve ends in her demanding, and receiving, Dragonstone as her own seat (seed row 97).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: several intervening stops (Ashemark, Castamere, the Golden Tooth, Wayfarer's Rest, Pinkmaiden) and a free grant decision by Jaehaerys separate Rhaena's resolve from the actual grant -- a disposition edge, not mechanical causation.

edges.quote: “Princess Aerea,” Lord Rogar answered at once. “There is a fire in her Jaehaerys does not have. She is young, but | can continue as her Hand, shape her, guide her, teach her all she must know. She has the stronger claim, her mother and father were King Aenys's first and secondborn, Jaehaerys was fourth.”
edges.note: Rogar's proposal to depose Jaehaerys and crown Aerea instead is met, in the same council session, by Alyssa's immediate dismissal of him as Hand (seed row 101).
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: same unbroken scene, nothing decisional between Rogar's proposal and Alyssa's response -- the very next beat.

edges.quote: One of those lost was Aerea Targaryen. On the night of Lord Rogar's dismissal, Ser Ronnal Baratheon and
edges.note: The text states explicitly that on the night of Rogar's dismissal, his brother Ronnal tried to seize Aerea from her chambers before the Baratheons quit the city.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: explicit same-night sequencing in the text ('On the night of Lord Rogar's dismissal').

edges.quote: only to find that Queen Alyssa had stolen a march on them. The girl was already gone, and her servants knew not where. It would be learned later that Lord Corbray had removed her, at the Queen Regent's command.
edges.note: Rogar's naming of Aerea as an alternative monarch in council put her in danger; Alyssa acted preemptively (via Lord Corbray) to spirit the girl away and hide her before the Baratheons could seize her.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Alyssa's protective action is her own decision, made in direct response to Rogar naming Aerea as claimant -- by the time Ronnal's men arrived, 'the girl was already gone.'

edges.quote: With Lord Rogar gone, Queen Alyssa named her brother Daemon Velaryon as Hand of the King, dispatched a raven to Dragonstone to tell her son Jaehaerys some (but not all) of what had occurred, and then retired to her chambers in Maegor's Holdfast.
edges.note: Immediately upon Rogar's departure, Alyssa names her brother Daemon Velaryon the new Hand.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'With Lord Rogar gone, Queen Alyssa named her brother...' -- the very next act in the same sentence, no intervening decision.

edges.quote: In war, he would boast, he would ne’er lay down his axe whilst life remained in his body...and this matter of the king’s marriage had become a war to him, one he was determined to win. One last folly remained to him, and he did not shrink from it.
edges.note: Humiliated by his dismissal and unable to accept defeat, Rogar attempts 'one last folly' -- sending his brother Orryn to seize the girl believed to be the true Aerea from the Oldtown motherhouse.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Rogar's decision to attempt the Oldtown ploy is a free (foolish) choice driven by the sting of his dismissal -- 'this matter of the king's marriage had become a war to him, one he was determined to win.'

edges.quote: On the twentieth day of the ninth moon of 50 AC, Jaehaerys Targaryen reached his sixteenth nameday and became a man grown. By the laws of the Seven Kingdoms, he was now old enough to rule in his own right, with no further need of a regent.
edges.note: The regency's legal end (Jaehaerys's sixteenth nameday) is the closing line of 'A Surfeit of Rulers'; 'A Time of Testing' opens immediately with the king's return to King's Landing to rule in his own right.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the majority is the section-break beat immediately followed by the king assuming personal rule -- same pattern as the Aegon III majority precedent named in the assignment notes.

edges.quote: The young king began his realm by remaking the council, keeping some men and replacing others who had proved unequal to their tasks.
edges.note: On his first day back, before anything else, Jaehaerys begins remaking the council.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: explicitly the king's first act upon return, no intervening decision.

edges.quote: All King’s Landing rejoiced when it was announced that Jaehaerys had also dismissed Edwell Celtigar as master of coin.
edges.note: Celtigar's failed, punishing taxes made him 'loathed throughout the city'; Jaehaerys's first act as king is to dismiss him as master of coin and strike down every one of his taxes.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Jaehaerys's choice to fire Celtigar is a free decision, driven by the taxes' visible failure -- the text plainly narrates the taxes' unpopularity immediately before narrating the dismissal that undoes them.

edges.quote: Only when all this had been done to his satisfaction and his new men were in place did Jaehaerys instruct
edges.note: Only once his own men are in place does Jaehaerys summon Rogar back to face him.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the text's own framing ('Only when all this had been done...did Jaehaerys instruct...') marks the council remaking as a satisfied precondition, not a forcing cause -- the summons and reconciliation remain Jaehaerys's free choice.

edges.quote: Across the width of Westeros, Rogar Baratheon fumed and raged when he learned of his brother's failure and imprisonment...but he did not call his banners, as many had feared. Instead he fell into despair.
edges.note: News of Orryn's failed ploy and imprisonment breaks Rogar's will to resist; he abandons any thought of calling his banners and instead awaits the king's judgment in despair.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the ploy's failure is the precondition that makes Rogar receptive when summoned to the throne room -- Jaehaerys's mercy remains his own free decision.

edges.quote: There, upon a canopied bed in Maegor's Holdfast in the Red Keep of King's Landing, the marriage of Jaehaerys Targaryen and his sister Alysanne was consummated at
edges.note: The public, consummated second wedding directly precedes Alysanne's pregnancy, the first fruit of the union.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: a natural, direct biological consequence of consummation, not mediated by a further free decision -- appropriate for the causal type rather than MOTIVATES.

edges.quote: With secrecy finally at an end, the king and his court waited to see how the realm would respond.
edges.note: With the marriage now public, Jaehaerys crafts (with Oswyck and, especially, Barth) the theological doctrine excusing Targaryen sibling marriage, and sends the Seven Speakers out to preach it.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the doctrine's creation is Jaehaerys's deliberate policy response to the marriage becoming known -- a free strategic choice, not an automatic effect.

edges.quote: We know the realm did not rise up against King Jaehaerys and Queen Alysanne in 51 AC as it had against Aegon and Rhaena ten years earlier. The why of it is a good deal less certain. The High Septon’s silence spoke loudly, no doubt, and the lords and common folk alike were weary
edges.note: Gyldayn credits the Doctrine of Exceptionalism and the Seven Speakers' preaching, alongside the High Septon's silence and the realm's war-weariness, as reasons no uprising greeted Jaehaerys and Alysanne's marriage as one had greeted Aegon and Rhaena's.
edges.rationale: CAUSES but tier-2/disputed: Gyldayn explicitly frames this as uncertain surmise among several possible causes ('The why of it is a good deal less certain... but... surely the Seven Speakers played a part as well'), not a flat assertion -- hedged historian's-judgment call.

edges.quote: Their uncle Maegor's taking of a second wife in 39 AC, in defiance of both the High Septon and his own brother, King Aenys, had shattered the delicate understanding between the Iron Throne and the Starry Sept, so the marriage of Aegon and Rhaena had been seen as a further outrage.
edges.note: Maegor's own second marriage in defiance of the High Septon (39 AC) is cited by Gyldayn as what had already broken the Iron Throne-Faith accord, making Aegon and Rhaena's later sibling marriage read as 'a further outrage' rather than an isolated affront.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: plainly narrated cause-effect ('had shattered... so the marriage... had been seen as a further outrage'). Per the assignment note: verified this stub is the real 39 AC/Sons-of-the-Dragon event, referenced here only in retrospect -- wired honestly using the grounding text actually present in this assigned unit rather than left unwired or mis-flagged.

edges.quote: “So be it. Give me Dragonstone and one thing more, and | shall trouble you no further.”
edges.note: In the same negotiation, Rhaena parlays her new grant of Dragonstone into a second demand -- the return of her daughter Aerea -- which Jaehaerys grants in the same breath.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Rhaena explicitly bundles the Aerea request onto the Dragonstone grant ('Give me Dragonstone and one thing more') -- the first concession is the leverage/precondition for the second, which remains Jaehaerys's own free 'Done.'

edges.quote: at Bitterbridge, he passed Septon Barth coming in the other direction. Barth was alone, riding on a donkey.
edges.note: Septon Mattheus's dismissal and Septon Barth's summons to the royal library are both part of Jaehaerys's council remaking; their legendary crossing at Bitterbridge marks the changeover.
edges.rationale: SUB_BEAT_OF, not causal: this is an anecdote illustrating (not causing) the broader council overhaul -- structural containment is the honest type here, per the rule that a stub may take a role/structural edge when nothing causal fits.

edges.quote: His son Walton succeeded him, and when a sudden rebellion broke out in 50 AC amongst the men of the Night's Watch at Rimegate and Sable Hall, he gathered his strength and rode to the Wall to join the leal watchmen in putting them down.
edges.note: Brandon Stark's death puts his son Walton in the lordship, giving him the authority to ride to the Wall and put down the Rimegate/Sable Hall rebellion in which Olyver Bracken is beheaded.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Brandon's death is a precondition (it puts Walton in the seat that lets him respond) rather than a forcing cause of either the uprising or the beheading.

edges.quote: Once joined by Lord Stark and his bannermen, the black brothers retook Rimegate and hanged the oathbreakers, save for Ser Olyver himself, who was beheaded by Lord Stark with his celebrated blade Ice.
edges.note: Bracken's beheading is the climax of the Rimegate half of the suppression of the Night's Watch rebellion of 50 AC.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: structural containment -- an incident within the named rebellion, matching the existing wiki-sourced hub node (which already had zero causal edges).

edges.quote: Brandon Stark's second son, Alaric, became the Lord of Winterfell. He would rule the North for twentythree years, an able man though a stern one...but for a long while he had no good to say of King Jaehaerys, for he blamed the king's clemency for his brother Walton's death,
edges.note: Walton Stark's death in the rebellion's aftermath (set upon by giants while pursuing the Sable Hall rebels beyond the Wall) passes the lordship to his brother Alaric.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: succession follows automatically and immediately upon Walton's death during the rebellion, nothing decisional between the two beats.

edges.quote: When the queen’s party reached the docks, however, they found Ser Franklyn waiting for them. The rest of them could go, and good
edges.note: Franklyn's decree that Rhaena and her court may leave but Elissa must stay behind is what he tries to enforce at the docks the next morning, sparking the crowd's uprising against him.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the dockside confrontation is the direct, next-morning enforcement of the banishment terms, with nothing decisional intervening before the crowd's spontaneous reaction.

edges.quote: None of these measures had the desired effect of filling up the treasury vaults. Instead building slowed to a halt, the inns emptied, and trade declined notably as merchants diverted their ships from King’s Landing to Driftmark, Duskendale, Maidenpool, and other ports where they might evade taxation.
edges.note: Celtigar's failed taxes drove trade away and emptied the treasury; construction on the Dragonpit itself halted for lack of funds -- the fiscal emergency Rego Draz is brought in to resolve (seed row 266).
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Celtigar's tax failure creates the treasury emergency, but the choice to seek foreign loans (rather than, say, raise taxes further) is Rego Draz and Jaehaerys's free policy decision.

edges.quote: The immediate need for gold was resolved by Rego Draz, the new master of coin, who reached out to the lron Bank of Braavos and its rivals in Tyrosh and Myr to arrange not one but three substantial loans.
edges.note: Rego Draz's three loans from the Iron Bank and its Tyroshi and Myrish rivals immediately fund the resumption of work on the Dragonpit (seed row 266).
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the text states this plainly and directly ('The securing of the loans had one immediate effect; work on the Dragonpit was able to resume') -- explicit, undisputed cause-effect.

edges.quote: Rosamund Ball took a dagger in the belly that, three days later, proved to be the death of her
edges.note: The Jonquil's Pool attack's dagger wound directly killed Rosamund Ball three days later.
edges.rationale: Direct physical wound-to-death mechanism narrated with no intervening decision; real causation through a short delay, so CAUSES rather than TRIGGERS (the death was not the literal next beat, it followed a three-day interval) or MOTIVATES (no human decision mediates the wound producing death).

edges.quote: Forever after, she blamed her son's death on the women who attacked her at Maidenpool.
edges.note: Alysanne herself blamed the Maidenpool attackers for her son Aegon's death three days after birth; this is her own grief-driven claim, not Gyldayn's objective assertion. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: The text explicitly frames this as Alysanne's personal belief ('she would say'), not the chronicler's own causal claim -- hedged, so tier-2 + disputed per Hard rule 5, routed as CAUSES on the in-universe claim rather than MOTIVATES since no further decision-event is the target.

edges.quote: But the young prince had come too early. Small and frail, he died three days after birth.
edges.note: Role edge grounding the newly minted death event to the infant prince, matching the PARTICIPATES_IN/VICTIM_IN pattern used for the other Jaehaerys-era births and deaths in this section.
edges.rationale: Role edge, not causal ladder; gives the new node its subject anchor.

edges.quote: Her Grace also held a women’s court at Gulltown, and a second at the Gates of the Moon;
edges.note: The Widow's Law stub (imposition-of-the-gate-fee's sibling) had zero causal edges; what Alysanne learned at the women's courts held during the king's Vale progress is what the text says directly produced the law. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: A human decision (Jaehaerys's, on Alysanne's counsel) mediates the link between what she learned and the decree, so MOTIVATES rather than CAUSES (agency-collapse check); routed to the event-target form since promulgation-of-the-widows-law is a specific downstream decision-event.

edges.quote: the challenge of paying for the completion of the Dragonpit and the rebuilding of King’s Landing strained even his talents. The taxes on silk, spice, and crenellations alone could not answer, so Lord Rego reluctantly imposed a new levy: a gate fee
edges.note: Stub wiring: the ongoing cost of finishing the Dragonpit (plus the city rebuild) is what the text says forced Rego Draz to impose the gate fee.
edges.rationale: Rego Draz's own decision to levy the fee mediates the financial pressure, so MOTIVATES (agency-collapse check) rather than CAUSES; sourced from the still-open Dragonpit construction project since no separate 'city rebuilding' node exists.

edges.rationale: Role edge, not causal ladder; gives the birth-of-corlys-velaryon stub its first edge.

edges.quote: Corlys Velaryon, the boy born on Driftmark in 53 AC, would take his ship the Sea Snake on nine great voyages, sailing farther than any man of Westeros had ever sailed before.
edges.note: Cross-section causal-spine link: Corlys's birth (section 10) is the precondition for his adult voyaging career (section 13), including reaching Asshai.
edges.rationale: Birth is a precondition, not a force compelling the later voyages (Corlys's own choices as a grown man determined he would sail) -- ENABLES preserves that agency rather than a false CAUSES.

edges.quote: the shipwrights of Braavos had completed work on the carrack Sun Chaser, the dream Elissa Farman had purchased with her stolen dragon's eggs.
edges.note: The gold from selling the three stolen dragon eggs to the Sealord funded the Sun Chaser's construction, which is what let Elissa Farman/Alys Westhill later depart on her voyage.
edges.rationale: The theft funded the ship (a precondition) but her decision to build and sail it was her own free choice, so ENABLES rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: the great fire that had swept across the Disputed Lands a fortnight past. Fanned by strong winds and fed by dry grasses, the blaze had raged for three days and three nights, engulfing half a dozen villages and one free company, the Adventurers, who found themselves trapped between the onrushing flames and a Tyroshi host under the command of the Archon himself.
edges.note: Stub wiring: no node previously existed for the wind-driven wildfire that trapped and destroyed the free company the Adventurers; minted great-fire-in-the-disputed-lands as the chain anchor.
edges.rationale: The fire's entrapment of the company between flames and a hostile army is a direct physical/military mechanism, not a mediating human decision by the victims, so CAUSES.

edges.quote: the magisters of Pentos were so unhappy with the terms that they sacrificed their prince to their queer gods, as is the custom of that city
edges.note: Stub wiring: the Pentoshi magisters' anger at the treaty terms is what the text says drove them to sacrifice their prince.
edges.rationale: The magisters' collective decision to sacrifice the prince mediates the link between the treaty and the sacrifice, so MOTIVATES (agency-collapse check) with the event as target rather than a false CAUSES; text states this as plain fact rather than a hedged rumor, so tier-1.

edges.quote: Lord Commander Burley also renamed Snowgate castle in her
edges.note: Stub wiring: Lord Commander Burley's renaming of Snowgate was an honor prompted by Alysanne's visit and patronage of the Night's Watch during her Northern progress. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Burley's own decision to rename the castle mediates the link, so MOTIVATES rather than CAUSES; routed to the event-target form.

edges.quote: The answer she proposed was a New Gift, a further strip of land south of Brandon's Gift.
edges.note: What Alysanne saw of the Night's Watch's poverty during her progress to the North is what the text says produced her proposal for the New Gift.
edges.rationale: Alaric Stark and the queen's own decisions mediate the doubling of the Gift, so MOTIVATES; both this and D-E12/D-E14 fan out from the same progress node, matching the roster's zero-causal-edge gap on queen-alysannes-progress-to-the-north.

edges.quote: Alysanne had anticipated that objection. She would pay for the castle herself,
edges.note: Alysanne's visit to Castle Black during her Northern progress is what led her to pledge her own jewels to fund the new castle, Deep Lake. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Her own decision to pay mediates the link, so MOTIVATES rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: she held her women’s court amongst the whores and strumpets of Mole’s Town...and there heard
edges.note: Marquee chain the text narrates at length: what Alysanne heard from the women of Mole's Town (the blacksmith's daughter's story above all) is what she brought to the small council, leading directly to the abolition of the first night. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Jaehaerys's council decision mediates the link between the testimony and the law, so MOTIVATES with the decision-event as target rather than a false CAUSES.

edges.quote: As their larders and granaries emptied, famine spread across the land, and old men bade farewell to their children and went out into the snow to die so their kin might live.
edges.note: Seed row 139: wires the winter-of-59-60-ac stub (previously zero edges despite bundling this whole chain into its own Identity prose) forward into a new famine-of-59-60-ac node, which also carries the hoarding/bread-price-rise consequence the text narrates in the same passage.
edges.rationale: Direct plain Gyldayn narration of cause and effect (failed crops/emptied larders producing famine); no human decision mediates the link, so CAUSES. Did not further split the famine into a separate 'bread prices' node -- that consequence is a market-level effect of the same famine, not a distinct narrated event, and is captured in the new node's identity to avoid over-minting.

edges.quote: One struck the Autumn Moon and split her mast from the crow's nest down to the deck.
edges.note: Stub wiring: minted storms-scatter-the-sun-chasers-fleet as the chain anchor for the three storms Eustace Hightower describes, since no node previously existed for them even though they produced two roster/stub events (this one and the islands' discovery).
edges.rationale: A lightning strike directly destroying the ship's mast, leading to her sinking, is a physical mechanism with no mediating decision, so CAUSES.

edges.quote: The winds and raging seas that had separated her Sun Chaser from the Hightowers had driven her westward, and when the dawn broke, her man in the crow's nest had espied birds circling a hazy mountain peak on the horizon.
edges.note: The same storms that sank the Autumn Moon also physically drove the Sun Chaser west onto the three uninhabited islands.
edges.rationale: Wind/current physically driving the ship, with the crew then simply sighting land, is a direct physical mechanism, not a human decision, so CAUSES.

edges.quote: So the voyagers had parted ways as they left the islands. Lady Meredith turned back east for home, whilst Alys Westhill and her Sun Chaser pressed on westward, chasing the sun.
edges.note: The islands let both battered ships resupply and repair; from there Alys Westhill chose to sail on alone into the unknown, never to be seen again.
edges.rationale: The islands were a precondition (repair/resupply stop) that made the onward voyage possible, but Alys Westhill's decision to press on west (over Eustace Hightower's refusal) is a free choice, so ENABLES rather than CAUSES (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: There he lost his love and half his crew, if the tales be true...and there as well, in Asshai’s harbor, he glimpsed an old and much weathered ship
edges.note: Reaching Asshai's harbor is the immediate occasion of Corlys's sighting of the ship he believed was the Sun Chaser -- same beat, no intervening steps. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break] [S204 adjudication: TRIGGERS->ENABLES — arrival is precondition for the sighting, not a spark]
edges.rationale: Arriving in the harbor and glimpsing the ship are effectively the same narrative moment with nothing decisional between them, so TRIGGERS; kept at tier-2/disputed matching the existing sun-chaser-glimpsed-in-asshais-harbor node's own hedge ('if the tales be true...he would swear').

edges.quote: Against the Shivers | was helpless,” he confessed to Septon Barth. “Against the Vulture, | was a king again.”
edges.note: Jaehaerys frames his zealous prosecution of Lord Rogar's War/the Vulture hunt as psychological compensation for his helplessness during the Shivers.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: the war's origin was Rogar Baratheon's request and the Vulture King's raiding, not the plague. The plague supplies the king's personal drive to embrace the war, a disposition-level motivation, not a forcing cause.

edges.quote: A day and a half after she had woken her mother from sleep complaining of feeling cold, the little princess was dead.
edges.note: Six-year-old Princess Daenerys dies of the Shivers within two days of falling ill.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: direct, unmediated disease death, a specific instance of the general epidemic already established by the plague node; no agency-collapse concern.

edges.quote: been attending council since the death of Princess Daenerys.
edges.note: Alysanne stops attending council after Daenerys's death.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targeting the character's changed behavior/disposition, the first documented instance of her withdrawing from governance after losing a child.

edges.quote: Amidst the chaos, His Grace would lose another of his lords, not to the Shivers but to ignorance and hate.
edges.note: The plague's collapse of the City Watch (commander Qarl Corbray dead of Shivers) creates the lawless conditions in which a hungry mob murders Rego Draz.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the epidemic is an explicit precondition (dead Watch commander, lawlessness, fear of foreigners blamed for the plague) but the mob's free choice to attack produced the murder; not a forced CAUSES.

edges.quote: Under torture, he soon gave up the names of the other attackers, and they were taken one and all.
edges.note: The king's manhunt after Rego Draz's murder directly produces the killers' capture and execution.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: real causation through the intermediate manhunt/interrogation steps, all directly narrated as one continuous royal response.

edges.quote: Vaegon is no maester...but he could well have the makings of an archmaester in him. The Citadel is the greatest repository of knowledge in the known world. Send him there.
edges.note: Grand Maester Elysar's counsel directly persuades Jaehaerys to send Vaegon to the Citadel instead of continuing the search for a bride.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Elysar's suggestion is the proximate driver of the king's decision ("his words struck home," three days later Vaegon is summoned and told he ships for Oldtown).

edges.quote: Plainly, the king and queen concluded afterward, some other bride must needs be found for Vaegon.
edges.note: Vaegon's public rejection of Daella ends the presumed sibling match and starts the years-long, ultimately failed, search for a bride that ends with Elysar's Citadel proposal.
edges.rationale: CAUSES through intermediate steps: the text explicitly narrates this as one continuous thread ("we shall return to Prince Vaegon...in due course", then resolves it years later at the Citadel decision).

edges.quote: “I would never marry her,” the boy said, in front of half the court.
edges.note: Vaegon is the agent of the public insult.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the new node's central actor.

edges.quote: Her sixteenth nameday was fast approaching, and with it her womanhood. Queen Alysanne was at her wit's end, and the king had lost his patience.
edges.note: The failed Blackwood match leaves Daella still unwed as her womanhood approaches, feeding the king's impatience that produces the year-end marriage ultimatum whose consequence (marriage, then pregnancy) kills her.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the collapse is a precondition for the mounting urgency, but Jaehaerys's forced-marriage ultimatum is the mediating free decision; preserves agency per the ladder rather than false CAUSES straight to her death.

edges.quote: It all fell to pieces when Daella learned that the Blackwoods kept the old gods,
edges.note: Daella's own refusal ends the courtship. [S204: quote trimmed at OCR blank-line break; continuation 'say her vows before a weirwood' is 2 lines down in the file]
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the new node's central actor.

edges.quote: Royce Blackwood was a gifted bowman, a fine swordsman, and a singer, who melted Daella’s heart with ballads of his own composition.
edges.note: Royce Blackwood was the suitor whose courtship collapsed.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the new node's second party.

edges.quote: first hint of the rift that would open between her and her king.
edges.note: Gyldayn identifies Daella's death as the first hint of the estrangement between Jaehaerys and Alysanne later called the First Quarrel / Great Rift.
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: the text calls it only the "first hint" — one contributing wound (alongside Alyssa's and Viserra's deaths and the Saera quarrel narrated later in the section) among several that together produce the eventual open rift; preserves the compound, multi-cause nature of the estrangement.

edges.quote: We ought never have pushed her into marriage.
edges.note: Alysanne directly blames Jaehaerys for insisting Daella marry — the seed of her later resentment.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the death drives Alysanne's changed disposition/blame toward the king, a character-target form distinct from E14-E12's event-target form.

edges.quote: In 93 AC, Prince Baelon’s sixteen-year-old son, Viserys, entered the Dragonpit and claimed Balerion.
edges.note: Stub wiring: Viserys is the agent claiming the dragon.
edges.rationale: No further causal claim is textually supported — Balerion's death less than a year later is presented as the old dragon's natural end, not a consequence of being ridden; role edge only, per section instructions ("causal only where text asserts it").

edges.quote: Only then did the king and queen send for Princess Saera.
edges.note: The overnight investigation triggered by the Blue Pearl discovery (interrogating Perianne, Alys, squires, and grooms) leads directly to Saera's own confrontation before the Iron Throne.
edges.rationale: CAUSES through the intermediate same-night interrogation the text narrates as one unbroken sequence; roster-flagged causally-dark node with a clean, explicit textual link.

edges.quote: A fortnight later, Jaehaerys and Alysanne gave another of their daughters over to the Faith. Princess Saera, who was not quite seventeen, departed King’s Landing for Oldtown, where her sister Septa Maegelle was to
edges.note: Saera's sentence at her judgment (confinement, then the Faith at Oldtown) places her at the motherhouse from which she later flees in 85 AC.
edges.rationale: CAUSES through intermediate steps (a year and a half of confinement at the motherhouse) explicitly narrated as the direct consequence of her judgment; roster-flagged causally-dark node.

edges.quote: Once his mourning for his wife and son had run its course, the king moved swiftly to resolve the longsimmering issue of the succession. Disregarding the precedents set by King Jaehaerys in 92 and the Great Council in 101, Viserys declared his daughter, Rhaenyra, to be his rightful heir, and named her Princess of Dragonstone.
edges.note: Aemma's death in childbed and the infant Baelon's death a day later remove Viserys's last hope of a son; he names Rhaenyra heir once mourning passes.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: text frames the declaration as the king's immediate next move once mourning ran its course, no intervening decisional beat.

edges.quote: The next occurred at High Tide after Ser Laenor’s funeral, when king and court made the journey to Driftmark to bear witness at his pyre, many on the backs of their dragons.
edges.note: Laenor's funeral is what brings the royal family, the Velaryon boys, and every dragon in the realm together at Driftmark, setting the stage for the brawl.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the funeral gathering is the precondition for the children being together at High Tide, not the cause of the brawl itself (a free choice by young Aemond).

edges.quote: Hold, Strong. First pay the debt you owe me.
edges.note: Aemond invokes the eye Lucerys took from him at Driftmark as the debt he means to collect at Storm's End, moments before pursuing Luke into the storm.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the old wound is Aemond's stated grievance/disposition driving his decision to demand blood, not a mechanical cause of the dragon fight.

edges.quote: “Was it one of your eyes he took, or one of your balls?” Maris asked the prince, in tones sweet as honey.
edges.note: Maris's jeer, after Aemond is turned away without bloodshed, is the immediate goad that sends him after Lucerys once outside Lord Borros's roof.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Maris's taunt is the proximate provocation for Aemond's decision to mount Vhagar and pursue Arrax, a free choice rather than a forced mechanical result.

edges.quote: died without waking. His body was discovered by a serving man at the hour of the bat, when it was the king’s custom to take a cup of hippocras. The servant ran to inform Queen Alicent, whose apartments were on the floor below
edges.note: The servant who finds the king's body reports directly and only to Queen Alicent, setting the concealment in motion within minutes of the death.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the discovery flows straight into Alicent's council with no decisional gap (the text explicitly frames the report-to-Alicent-alone as instructed in advance).

edges.quote: Drawing his dagger, the Clubfoot drew it across his palm. “A blood oath,” he urged, “to bind us all together, brothers unto death.” And so each of the conspirators slashed their palms and clasped hands with one another, swearing brotherhood.
edges.note: The oath is Larys Strong's act within the same all-night council session that conceals Viserys's death and resolves to crown Aegon; it is a beat inside that hub, not a separate event.
edges.rationale: SUB_BEAT_OF: verified the existing node is specifically the oath-swearing moment, not the whole concealment-and-succession-plotting session, which needed its own anchor (green-council-conceals-death-of-viserys, minted here).

edges.quote: The bells began to ring on the tenth day of the third moon of 129 AC, tolling the end of a reign. Grand Maester Orwyle was at last allowed to send forth his ravens, and the black birds took to the air by the hundreds, spreading the word of Aegon’s ascension to every far corner of the realm.
edges.note: The green council's all-night planning session (secrecy, arrests, alliances) culminates directly in the public announcement and coronation of Aegon. [S204 adjudication: CAUSES->ENABLES — Aegon resisted; Cole's threat mediated]
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the coronation is the council's own collective decision executed through preparation, not a further free choice by an outside third party.

edges.quote: The child had not been due for another turn of the moon, but the tidings from King’s Landing had driven the princess into a black fury, and her rage seemed to bring on the birth, as if the babe inside her were angry too, and fighting to get out.
edges.note: News of Aegon's crowning reaching Dragonstone brings on Rhaenyra's early, fatal labor.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the text states the tidings directly brought on the premature birth, an immediate physiological effect with no decisional step between them.

edges.quote: her pain. “She was my only daughter, and they killed her. They stole my crown and murdered my daughter, and they shall answer for it.”
edges.note: Rhaenyra's own vow over her dead daughter (framed as both crown-theft and child-murder) is her stated motive for answering with her own coronation and war.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form): Rhaenyra's grief and fury is a personal disposition driving her subsequent free decision to crown herself, not a mechanical cause.

edges.quote: They stole my crown and murdered my daughter, and they shall answer for it.
edges.note: Aegon's crowning in King's Landing is answered directly by Rhaenyra convening her own council and, the next day, her own coronation. [S204 adjudication: re-quoted to the crown-theft vow line — grounds usurpation->counter-coronation directly]
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Aegon's coronation does not force Rhaenyra's counter-coronation, it is the provocation her own council freely chooses to answer with a rival crowning.

edges.quote: A hasty coronation was held the next day. The arrival of Ser Steffon Darklyn, late of Aegon's Kingsguard, was an occasion of much joy on Dragonstone, especially when it was learned that he and his fellow loyalists (“turncloaks,” Ser Otto would name them, when offering a reward for their capture) had brought the stolen crown of King Jaehaerys the Conciliator.
edges.note: Darklyn's defection from the Kingsguard delivers the stolen crown of Jaehaerys the Conciliator, the very crown Daemon places on Rhaenyra's head.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the stolen crown makes the specific ceremony possible as staged (an ancestral crown, not an improvised one) but does not force the coronation, which the black council had already resolved on.

edges.quote: “We have friends in the riverlands,” the prince said, “though not all of them dare show their colors yet. We need a place where they can gather, a toehold on the mainland large enough to house a sizable host, and strong enough to hold against
edges.note: At the same Dragonstone council that crowns Rhaenyra, Daemon argues the blacks need a mainland toehold for their scattered riverland sympathizers and proposes Harrenhal.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form): the council's strategic need is Daemon's stated reason for the assault, which he then personally chooses to lead — a free decision, not a forced mechanical consequence.

edges.quote: “Find riders to master Silverwing, Vermithor, and Seasmoke, and we will have nine dragons against Aegon’s four. Mount and fly their wild kin, and we will number twelve, even without Stormcloud,”
edges.note: Rhaenys's dragonseed strategy, laid out at Rhaenyra's black council, is the war plan that later leads riders to be sought for the wild dragons around Dragonstone, including Sheepstealer.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the council's decision to seek riders for wild/unclaimed dragons is a precondition for Nettles later taming Sheepstealer, but does not force that specific outcome (a free act by a specific rider, chapters later).

edges.quote: It was decided that Jace, being the older of the two, would take the longer, more dangerous task, flying first to the Eyrie to treat with the Lady of the Vale, then to White Harbor to win over Lord Manderly, and lastly to Winterfell to meet with Lord Stark.
edges.note: The black council's decision to send Jacaerys as envoy is what puts him in the Eyrie's High Hall to win Lady Jeyne Arryn's allegiance.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: being sent as envoy is the precondition for the negotiation; Jeyne Arryn's actual choice to ally is her own, so the council's decision does not force the outcome.

edges.quote: gave the queen a stronghold in the heart of Westeros, to which her supporters could rally... and Rhaenyra had many such in the lands watered by the Trident. When Prince Daemon sent forth his call to arms, they rose up all along the rivers, knights and men-at-arms and humble peasants who yet remembered the Realm’s Delight, so beloved of her father, and the way she smiled and charmed them as she made her progress through the riverlands in her youth.
edges.note: The bloodless fall of Harrenhal gives Daemon a rallying point, and his call to arms from that captured seat brings the riverlands up for Rhaenyra en masse.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: text states plainly that the captured stronghold plus the call to arms produced the mass rising, real causation through the intermediate step of Daemon's summons rather than a third party's independent choice.

edges.quote: As the black council sat to consider how to strike back, a raven arrived from Harrenhal. “An eye for an eye, a son for a son,” Prince Daemon wrote. “Lucerys shall be avenged.”
edges.note: Daemon's raven vowing a son-for-a-son reprisal for Luke's death is the direct order that sets Blood and Cheese's mission in motion.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (event-target form): Luke's death does not mechanically cause the murder; Daemon's free decision to demand vengeance mediates, and a specific downstream decision-event (the murder) exists, so the event-target form is preferred over collapsing agency into CAUSES.

edges.quote: the hulking swordsman slew Prince Jaehaerys, striking off the boy’s head with a single blow
edges.note: Blood is the one who strikes the killing blow.
edges.rationale: Role edge to give the new event hub its perpetrator; mirrors the existing blood-butcher KILLS jaehaerys-targaryen-son-of-aegon-ii dyad.

edges.quote: Cheese appeared to snatch up Maelor
edges.note: Cheese is Blood's accomplice who guides the pair into the castle, seizes Maelor, and forces Helaena's choice.
edges.rationale: Role edge for the second perpetrator, completing the hub alongside blood-butcher.

edges.quote: the hulking swordsman slew Prince Jaehaerys, striking off the boy’s head with a single blow
edges.note: Prince Jaehaerys, Aegon II's heir, is the boy killed.
edges.rationale: Role edge naming the victim, standard hub-completion pattern.

edges.quote: Queen Helaena kept her calm, it is said.
edges.note: Helaena is present throughout, forced to choose which child dies, and present at Jaehaerys's death; the traumatic aftermath (her scream) is narrated in the same passage.
edges.rationale: WITNESS_IN: she does not act against the killers, she is present and forced to watch/choose — the correct role for the graph's downstream-effect-on-Helaena ask.

edges.quote: In his grief and fury, King Aegon II commanded that all the city's ratcatchers be taken out and hanged, and this was done.
edges.note: Aegon II's grief and fury over his son's murder drives his order to hang every ratcatcher in the city (Cheese's trade), even though Cheese himself was never caught.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: an explicit emotional/political decision by the king in reaction to the murder, not a forced mechanical outcome.

edges.quote: And with his death, the war of ravens and envoys and marriage pacts came to an end, and the war
edges.note: Luke's death is the hinge the text itself names as ending the diplomatic phase and opening the fighting phase of the Dance.
edges.rationale: PART_OF (war containment): a marquee battle-adjacent death explicitly framed by the text as a phase-change within the war.

edges.quote: It was a war marked by stealth, murder, and betrayal as well, a war fought in shadows and stairwells, council chambers and castle yards with knives and lies
edges.note: The Dance's own defining description (a war of stairwells, knives, and stealth murder) is exactly what the Blood and Cheese killing is.
edges.rationale: PART_OF: the assassination is a component atrocity of the war, explicitly of the kind the book's own framing passage calls out.

edges.quote: Finally Vermax carried Jacaerys Velaryon to Winterfell, to treat with its formidable young lord, Cregan Stark.
edges.note: Jace's envoy mission continues from the Eyrie and White Harbor on to Winterfell, where the Pact of Ice and Fire is struck with Cregan Stark.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the same mission that won the Vale is what carries Jace onward to Winterfell, but Cregan's agreement is his own free choice, not forced by the earlier leg's success.

edges.quote: First to pay that price were the captive lords languishing in the dungeons under the Red Keep, men who had once sworn to defend the rights of Princess Rhaenyra and still stubbornly refused to bend the knee to King Aegon.
edges.note: Criston Cole's new hard line as Hand opens with the immediate execution of the captive black lords.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the executions are the very next narrated beat after Cole's 'steel fist' proclamation, with nothing decisional between the appointment and the killings.

edges.quote: “We will pay the princess back in her own bloody coin,” he told the king.
edges.note: As Hand and Lord Commander, Cole devises the covert-assassin mission that leads to the Cargyll duel.
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: Cole's plan makes the fatal encounter possible, but it was chance ('the gods brought him face-to-face with Ser Erryk himself') that actually produced the duel, not Cole's design forcing it.

edges.quote: “You must rule the realm now, until your brother is strong enough to take the crown again,” the King’s Hand told Prince Aemond.
edges.note: Aegon's Rook's Rest maiming leaves him unable to rule, so Cole hands the regency to Aemond.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targets the decision-event: Aegon's incapacitation is the reason, but Cole's proclamation is the human decision that actually installs Aemond as regent.

edges.quote: With Sunfyre wounded near Rook's Rest and unable to fly, and Tessarion with Prince Daeron in Oldtown, only two mature dragons remained to defend King’s Landing
edges.note: Sunfyre's Rook's Rest wounding leaves the greens dragon-poor, and Jacaerys reasons the blacks can now overwhelm Vhagar if he can field enough riders — the impetus for the Sowing.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the battle's outcome drives Jacaerys's decision to call the dragonseeds, rather than mechanically forcing the Sowing itself.

edges.quote: In the end, the brown dragon was brought to heel by the cunning and persistence of a “small brown girl” of six-and-ten, who delivered him a freshly slaughtered sheep every morning, until Sheepstealer learned to accept and expect her.
edges.note: Nettles' taming of Sheepstealer is one strand of the wider Sowing call for dragonriders.
edges.rationale: SUB_BEAT_OF: Nettles' individual taming is one beat inside the larger named Sowing event, not an independent causal chain.

edges.quote: As Silverwing, Sheepstealer, Seasmoke, and Vermithor descended upon them, the men of the Triarchy felt their courage desert them.
edges.note: The four new dragonriders produced by the Sowing are what breaks the Triarchy fleet's line in the Gullet.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the Sowing supplies the extra dragons that make the naval rout possible; it does not by itself force the battle or its outcome.

edges.quote: The sight of the queen’s dragons in the sky above took the heart out of the opposition, and King Aegon’s remaining loyalists hid
edges.note: Sowing-produced dragonriders (Sheepstealer, Silverwing, Vermithor, Seasmoke) land on the city's hills as a show of force during the fall of King's Landing.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the extra dragons make the bloodless collapse of resistance possible, but the actual fall is forced by the City Watch's defection, a separate free decision.

edges.quote: Not long after Addam of Hull had proved himself by flying Seasmoke, Lord Corlys went so far as to petition Queen Rhaenyra to remove the taint of bastardy from him and his brother.
edges.note: Addam's Sowing success directly prompts Corlys's petition to legitimize him and Alyn.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the petition follows immediately ('not long after') from Addam mounting Seasmoke, with no other intervening event.

edges.quote: Finally one quarrel took him through the neck, and Jace was swallowed by the sea.
edges.note: Jacaerys dies in the water during the Gullet fighting after Vermax goes down.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: his death is the immediate next beat after Vermax's crash, within the same continuous action.

edges.quote: Spicetown was brutally sacked, the bodies of men, women, and children butchered in the streets and left as fodder for gulls and rats and carrion crows, its buildings burned. The town would never be rebuilt.
edges.note: Marquee Gullet consequence: Spicetown sacked and never rebuilt.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the location as a casualty of the battle hub, per the assignment's call to wire the Gullet's marquee consequences.

edges.quote: High Tide was put to the torch as well. All the treasures the Sea Snake had brought back from the east were consumed by fire, his servants cut down as they tried to flee the flames.
edges.note: Marquee Gullet consequence: High Tide burned and looted of the Sea Snake's treasures.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the location as a casualty of the battle hub, per the assignment's call to wire the Gullet's marquee consequences.

edges.quote: for the rank and file of the City Watch still loved Daemon Targaryen, the Prince of the City who had commanded them of old.
edges.note: The gold cloaks' lingering loyalty to Daemon drives their choice to imprison/kill Aegon's loyalist gate captains and open the city. [S204 adjudication: MOTIVATES->ENABLES — the Watch is the actor; its loyalty is the door-opener]
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the Watch's disposition is what drives their free decision to betray Aegon and hand the city to Rhaenyra.

edges.quote: and ordered the city gates opened to the men pouring off the Sea Snake's ships.
edges.note: Luthor Largent kills Gwayne Hightower and, in the same breath, opens the gates to the blacks.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the gate-opening order is the same act, same sentence, immediately following Gwayne's killing.

edges.quote: Ser Otto Hightower, who had served three kings as Hand, was the first traitor to be beheaded.
edges.note: Once the city falls and Rhaenyra takes the throne, Otto is the first green lord executed for treason.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: Otto's execution is part of the immediate settling of accounts that follows directly on the city's capture.

edges.quote: Small wonder then that when word of the fall of King's Landing reached him, the prince felt thrice the fool. His fury was fearsome to behold.
edges.note: News of the city's fall enrages Aemond at Harrenhal, and he vents that fury on the Strongs, whom he brands traitors.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'First to suffer for it was Ser Simon Strong' follows in the very next sentence, with Aemond's fury as the direct, undeliberated spark.

edges.quote: Willow Pound-Stone tore the weeping prince from his arms.
edges.note: After Ser Rickard Thorne is shot down on the bridge, the mob seizes Prince Maelor from his arms.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the boy's seizure by the mob is the immediate next beat after Thorne's death, in the same continuous scene.

edges.quote: In a voice as cold as ice, she commanded Ser Luthor Largent to take twenty gold cloaks to the Dragonpit and arrest Ser Addam Velaryon.
edges.note: Mysaria's accusation against Nettles hardens Rhaenyra's suspicion of the dragonseeds generally, and she orders Addam's arrest, which he flees.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targets the decision-event: the accusation drives Rhaenyra's arrest order, and Addam's own choice to flee (once forewarned) is a further free decision, so TRIGGERS/CAUSES would collapse two layers of human agency.

edges.quote: Balked and angry, Ser Luthor returned at once to the Red Keep, where he burst into the Tower of the Hand and laid rough hands on the aged Lord Corlys, accusing him of treachery.
edges.note: Frustrated by Addam's escape, Luthor Largent immediately arrests Corlys instead.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'at once' signals the same unbroken scene, Luthor's reflexive redirection of his warrant onto Corlys.

edges.quote: Let him take her at table or abed and strike her head off. Only then shall
edges.note: Mysaria's accusation leads Rhaenyra to send a kill warrant for Nettles to Maidenpool.
