edges.rationale: ENABLES: the kill order creates the threat that produces Nettles' flight, but it does not force it — Maester Norren's free decision to warn Daemon and Nettles is what actually produces the departure (see G17-E21).

edges.quote: found Prince Daemon and the girl Nettles at their supper that night, and showed them the queen's letter.
edges.note: Maester Norren chooses to warn Daemon and Nettles of the queen's kill order rather than deliver it silently, prompting their flight from Maidenpool the next dawn.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Norren's warning is the act that drives Daemon and Nettles' decision to depart at first light; it is his free choice against his own vows, not a mechanical trigger.

edges.quote: No longer tied to castle or host, the oneeyed prince was free to fly where he would.
edges.note: Aemond's unrestricted, solo raiding campaign is what eventually brings Vhagar within Alys Rivers's 'sight' of Harrenhal for the duel with Daemon.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Aemond's freedom of movement is the precondition for the eventual confrontation, but he still has to choose to answer the challenge at Harrenhal.

edges.quote: You were a fool to come alone.
edges.note: With Nettles and Sheepstealer gone, Daemon is alone at Harrenhal with only Caraxes — exactly what lets Aemond risk the one-on-one duel.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Nettles' departure is the precondition that leaves Daemon without a second dragon; the text has Aemond himself name Daemon's solitude as decisive ('You were a fool to come alone'), while Aemond's choice to actually attack remains his own free decision.

edges.quote: “Were | not alone, you would not have come,” said Daemon.
edges.note: Daemon's solitary vigil at Harrenhal was deliberate bait, by his own admission, to draw Aemond into single combat.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Daemon's own words confirm his occupation and lone wait at Harrenhal was a calculated lure, driving Aemond's decision to come and fight rather than mechanically forcing it.

edges.quote: Helaena had been murdered, the whispers went, as her sons had been before her. Prince Daeron and his dragons would soon be at the gates, and with them the end of Rhaenyra’s reign. The old queen was determined that her young half-sister should not live to revel in her downfall, so she had sent Ser Luthor Largent to seize
edges.note: Helaena's suicide + the murder rumor ignites the bloody riot that night.
edges.rationale: Same-night, no decisional agent mediates a mass uprising; text states the riot begins the very night the rumor spreads (line 41: 'That night King's Landing rose in bloody riot'), so TRIGGERS over CAUSES.

edges.quote: Even the fearsome Ser Luthor Largent could not escape the carnage. His sword torn from his grasp, Largent was pulled from his saddle, stabbed in the belly, and bludgeoned to death with a cobblestone
edges.note: Luthor Largent and the City Watch are annihilated in Cobbler's Square during night-one of the riot.
edges.rationale: Not a separate causal step; it is a beat inside the same riot-night event, hence SUB_BEAT_OF not CAUSES.

edges.quote: No such rescuers came for Lord Bartimos Celtigar, whose walled manse was defended only by six guardsmen and a few hastily armed servants. When rioters came swarming over the walls, these dubious defenders threw down their weapons and ran, or joined the attackers. Arthor Celtigar, a boy of fifteen, made a brave stand in a doorway, sword in hand, and kept the howling mob at bay for a few moments...until a treacherous serving girl let the rioters in through a back way. The brave lad was slain by a spear thrust through the back. Lord Bartimos himself fought his way to the stables, only to find all his horses dead or stolen. Taken, the queen's despised master of coin was bound to a post and tortured until he revealed where all his wealth was hidden. Then a tanner called Wat announced that his lordship had failed to pay his “cock tax," and must
edges.note: Rioters storm and loot the Celtigar manse, torturing and mutilating Lord Bartimos, during the same riot.
edges.rationale: A concurrent beat within the riot's geography, not an independent causal link — SUB_BEAT_OF.

edges.quote: In a brothel on the Street of Silk, the whores raised up their own king, a palehaired boy of four named Gaemon, supposedly a bastard of the missing King Aegon II. Not to be outdone, a hedge knight named Ser Perkin the Flea crowned his own squire Trystane, a stripling of sixteen years, declaring him to be a natural son of the late King Viserys. Any knight can make a knight, and when Ser Perkin began dubbing every sellsword, thief, and butcher's boy who flocked to Trystane’s ragged banner, men and boys
edges.note: The collapse of order during the riot lets the Street of Silk whores raise up Gaemon as a mummer-king.
edges.rationale: The riot is a precondition (breakdown of authority) that makes the opportunistic crowning possible but doesn't force it — the whores' choice is a free decision. ENABLES preserves that agency.

edges.quote: The crowd, Septon Eustace says, was twice as large and thrice as fearful as the night before. Like the queen they so despised, the Shepherd’s “lambs” were looking to the sky with dread, fearing that King Aegon’s dragons would arrive before the night was out, with an army close behind them. No longer
edges.note: Night-one's riot leaves the second-night crowd at Cobbler's Square twice as large and thrice as fearful, priming it for the march on the Dragonpit.
edges.rationale: A precondition/amplifier (bigger, more frightened crowd) rather than the direct spark — that role belongs to the Shepherd's sermon (see H-E06). Tier-2 because explicitly sourced to Eustace ('Septon Eustace says').

edges.quote: “There the demons dwell, up there. Fire and blood, blood and fire. This is their city. If you would make it yours, first must you destroy them. If you would
edges.note: The Shepherd's second-night sermon directly incites the crowd to march on the Dragonpit and kill the dragons.
edges.rationale: Immediate spark — the very next beat is the crowd's cry ('Kill them! Kill them!') and the march begins; no decisional gap between the sermon and the mob's action.

edges.quote: King’s Landing was lost, all agreed; they must needs abandon the city. Reluctantly, Her Grace was persuaded to leave the next day, at dawn. With the Mud Gate in the hands of her foes, and all the ships along the river burned or sunk, Rhaenyra and a small band of followers slipped out through the Dragon Gate, intending to make their way up the coast to Duskendale. With her rode the brothers Manderly, four surviving Queensguard, Ser Balon Byrch
edges.note: The loss of the pit dragons, Syrax, and Prince Joffrey convinces Rhaenyra's counselors the city is lost, motivating her decision to flee.
edges.rationale: A human decision (Rhaenyra's counselors persuading her, and her own reluctant agreement) mediates the loss of the city and the flight — agency-collapse check routes this via MOTIVATES rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: Seven men did ride down from the Red Keep that night, into the madness of the city. Munkun tells us they were men of honor, duty bound to obey their queen’s commands. Septon Eustace would have us believe that their hearts had been touched by a mother's love for her son. Mushroom names them dolts and dastards, eager for some rich reward, and “too dull to believe that they might die.” For once it may be that all three of our
edges.note: Prince Joffrey's theft of Syrax and fall from her back during the storming immediately sends seven knights riding into the riot to recover him.
edges.rationale: Immediate, same-night response with no intervening decision beyond the queen's frantic command — TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: Her Grace had not been gone half a day when Ser Perkin the Flea and his gutter knights appeared outside the gates, demanding that the castle yield. Though outnumbered ten to one, the queen's garrison might still have resisted, but Ser Garth chose instead to strike Rhaenyra’s banners, open his gates, and trust to the mercy of the foe.
edges.note: Rhaenyra's departure leaves the Red Keep undermanned, and Ser Garth chooses to yield it to Ser Perkin's gutter knights within a day.
edges.rationale: Her flight is a precondition (removes the best defenders) but Ser Garth's choice to yield rather than resist ten-to-one odds is his own decision — ENABLES, not CAUSES.

edges.quote: The Flea proved to have no mercy. Garth the Harelip was dragged before him and beheaded, along with
edges.note: Having taken the castle, Ser Perkin the Flea has Garth the Harelip beheaded despite the yielding.
edges.rationale: Perkin's choice to show 'no mercy' is an explicit decision by an agent who now controls the outcome — MOTIVATES the execution rather than a forced automatic consequence.

edges.quote: Both were on hand the next day to bear witness as Ser Perkin's gangling squire Trystane mounted the Iron Throne. So too was the Queen Dowager, Alicent of House Hightower. Down in the black cells, Ser Perkin’s men even found King Aegon’s former master of coin, Ser Tyland Lannister, still alive...though Rhaenyra’s torturers had blinded him, pulled out his fingernails and toenails, cut off his ears, and relieved him
edges.note: Once the Red Keep yields, Ser Perkin's squire Trystane is free to sit the actual Iron Throne the next day.
edges.rationale: Controlling the castle is the precondition that makes sitting the throne possible; it doesn't force it (Perkin could have installed himself) — ENABLES.

edges.quote: Half of her gold cloaks deserted on the road, and one night her camp was attacked by broken men. Though her knights beat off the attackers, Ser Balon Byrch was felled by an arrow, and Ser Lyonel Bentley, a young knight of the Queensguard, suffered a blow to the head that cracked his helm. He
edges.note: Byrch felled and Bentley mortally wounded when broken men attack Rhaenyra's camp during her march toward Duskendale.
edges.rationale: An incident within the same flight-journey narrated by the flight-to-dragonstone node, not an independent causal step — SUB_BEAT_OF.

edges.quote: She was adamant on returning to Dragonstone. There she would find dragon's eggs, she told her loyalists; she must
edges.note: Rhaenyra's own choice to make specifically for Dragonstone (to find dragon's eggs) is what puts her in her brother's trap.
edges.rationale: Her decision to head to Dragonstone is a precondition for the ambush there but does not by itself force her death (she does not yet know Aegon holds the castle) — ENABLES.

edges.quote: The blood drained from the queen's cheeks when she beheld the bodies, but young Prince Aegon was the
edges.note: Aegon II's prior seizure of Dragonstone from its garrison is what turns Rhaenyra's homecoming into an ambush.
edges.rationale: A precondition (Aegon already controls the castle when she lands) that makes her death possible without itself forcing it (Aegon still chooses to feed her to Sunfyre) — ENABLES.

edges.quote: At the king's command, the chain that Princess Rhaenyra had torn from Grand Maester Orwyle’s neck and given to Gerardys was now used to hang him. He was not given the quick end of a hard fall and a broken neck, but rather a slow strangulation, kicking as he gasped for air. Thrice, when he was almost dead, Gerardys was
edges.note: Immediately after seizing the castle, King Aegon II has Grand Maester Gerardys hanged and disemboweled.
edges.rationale: Same coup-night sequence, direct royal command with no separate deliberation narrated — TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: “You fed his mother to your dragon,” she reminded her son. “The boy saw it all.”
edges.note: Alicent reminds Aegon II that young Prince Aegon witnessed his mother's death by dragon — the formative trauma behind the boy who will become the melancholy 'Dragonbane.'
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targets the character/disposition per the ladder's guidance (an event shaping a person rather than forcing a discrete downstream event) — Aegon III's grief and dragon-aversion (see harvest) trace directly to this witnessed death.

edges.quote: Seasmoke’s descent upon Riverrun had at last persuaded that reluctant warrior, Ser Elmo Tully, to call his banners for the queen, in defiance of the wishes of his bedridden grandsire, Lord Grover. “A dragon in one’s courtyard does wonders to resolve one's doubts,” Ser Elmo is reported to have said. The great host encamped about the walls of Tumbleton outnumbered the attackers, but they had been too long in one place. Their discipline had grown lax (drunkenness was endemic in the camp, Grand Maester Munkun says, and disease had taken root as well), the death of Lord Ormund Hightower had left them without a leader, and the lords who wished to command in his place were at odds with one another. So intent were they upon their own conflicts and rivalries that they had all but forgotten their true foes. Ser Addam’s night attack took them completely unawares. Before the men of Prince Daeron’s army even knew they were in a battle, the enemy was amongst them, cutting them down as they staggered from their tents, as they were saddling their
edges.note: Seasmoke's landing at Riverrun persuades the reluctant Elmo Tully to call his banners, swelling Addam Velaryon's relief army to nearly 4,000.
edges.rationale: Tully's banners are one contributing precondition (troop strength) among several for the coming battle, not its sole cause — ENABLES. (Resolves seed 38; its packet slug_candidates were wrong-era false positives — see flags.)

edges.quote: That was more than Prince Daeron's loyalist lords were prepared to suffer. Lord Unwin Peake and a somewhat reluctant Hobert Hightower summoned eleven other lords and landed knights to a secret council in the cellar of a Tumbleton inn, to discuss what might be done to curb the arrogance of the baseborn dragonriders. The plotters agreed that it would be a simple matter to dispose of White, who was drunk more oft than not and had never shown any great prowess at arms. Hammer posed a greater danger, for of late he was surrounded day and night by lickspittles, camp followers, and sellswords eager for his favor. It would serve them little to kill White and leave Hammer alive, Lord Peake pointed out; Hard Hugh must needs die first. Long and loud were the arguments in the inn beneath the sign reading “the Bloody Caltrops,” as the lords discussed
edges.note: Hammer's self-coronation and his horseshoe-nailing of Ser Roger Corne is what finally drives Prince Daeron's loyalist lords to convene the Caltrops plot.
edges.rationale: A deliberate decision by named lords ('summoned eleven other lords... to a secret council') mediates the outrage and the plot — MOTIVATES, not CAUSES.

edges.quote: “You died in the battle,” Bold Jon replied, drawing OrphanMaker
edges.note: Caltrops conspirator Jon Roxton guts Hammer in the chaos of the Second Battle of Tumbleton, exactly as the plot intended.
edges.rationale: The council's resolution to kill Hammer first (Roxton the designated striker) is realized nearly verbatim ('You died in the battle') — real causation, via the intermediate step of the surprise attack providing cover.

edges.quote: Half-dressed, he rushed down the steps to the yard, calling for his hammer, his armor, and a horse, so he might ride out and mount Vermithor. His men rushed to obey, even as Seasmoke set the stables ablaze. But Lord Jon Roxton had claimed Lord Footly’s bedchamber along with Lord Footly's wife, and was already in the
edges.note: Seasmoke's surprise attack and burning stables force Hammer into the yard half-dressed, where Roxton is waiting.
edges.rationale: The battle's chaos is the enabling circumstance/opportunity for the assassination, distinct from and additional to the plot itself (H-E21) — ENABLES.

edges.quote: And though Hard Hugh Hammer had died, and his dreams of kingship with him, the second Betrayer remained. Ulf White had woken from his
edges.note: With Hammer dead, the surviving Caltrops (Hobert Hightower foremost) turn on the second Betrayer, Ulf White, and poison his wine the next morning.
edges.rationale: Direct continuation of the same conspiracy's stated goal (kill both dragonriders) through the intermediate step of Hobert's visit with poisoned wine — CAUSES.

edges.quote: Betrayals and betrothals followed, until an accord was reached between Lord Borros, Lord Larys, and Queen Alicent, with Grand Maester Orwyle as witness. The Clubfoot promised that Ser Perkin and his gutter knights would join the stormlanders in restoring King Aegon II to the Iron Throne, on the condition that all of them save the pretender Trystane would be pardoned for any and all offenses, including high treason, rebellion, robbery,
edges.note: The Storm's End tent accord's central term is restoring Aegon II to the Iron Throne; the deal with Borros is what clears the crownlands and city for his return.
edges.rationale: The accord is the political precondition; Aegon's return still depends on further separate steps (Corlys's submission, ships) so it enables rather than directly causing the return. Resolves seed 48.

edges.quote: The problem posed by the Velaryon fleet was discussed at some length. “We must bring the Sea Snake into this,” Lord Baratheon is reported to have said. “Perhaps the old man would like a new young wife. | have two
edges.note: The Velaryon-fleet problem is raised and resolved to pursue in the same Storm's End tent parley, setting up the later Corlys negotiation.
edges.rationale: The accord opens the door to bringing Corlys onside (a separate negotiation, not forced) — ENABLES.

edges.quote: he rode forth the next day against Visenya’s Hill and the “Cunny King,” Gaemon Palehair. Columns of armored knights climbed the hill from three directions, riding down the street scum, sellswords, and drunkards who had gathered round the little king and putting them to rout. The young monarch, who had celebrated his fifth nameday only two days previous, was carried back to the Red Keep slung over the back of a horse, chained and weeping. His mother walked behind him, clutching the hand of the Dornishwoman Sylvenna Sand and leading a long column of whores, witch women, cutpurses, sneaks, and sots, the surviving remnants of the Palehair “court.”
edges.note: Empowered by the Storm's End accord and now controlling the city gates, Borros rides against Gaemon Palehair's 'court' the very next day.
edges.rationale: The accord (which restores Borros's authority in the city) is the precondition for his ability to act against Visenya's Hill — ENABLES.

edges.quote: The young monarch, who had celebrated his fifth nameday only two days previous, was carried back to the Red Keep slung over the back of a horse, chained and weeping. His mother walked behind him, clutching the hand of the Dornishwoman Sylvenna Sand and leading a long column of whores, witch women, cutpurses, sneaks, and sots, the surviving remnants of the Palehair “court.”
edges.note: Gaemon's mock kingdom atop Visenya's Hill is the very target Borros crushes.
edges.rationale: Gaemon's 'court' existing is a precondition for there being anything to rout; a second, independent precondition alongside the accord (H-E26) — ENABLES.

edges.quote: Escorted by twelve Velaryon war galleys, they sailed upon a battered old trading cog named Mouse, owned and captained by Marilda of Hull. If Mushroom may be trusted, the choice of vessel was deliberate. “Lord Alyn might have shipped the king home aboard Lord Aethan’s Glory or Morning Tide or even Spicetown Girl, but he wanted him seen to be creeping into the city on a mouse,” the dwarf says. “Lord Alyn was
edges.note: Corlys's pardon and submission puts twelve Velaryon war galleys at Aegon II's disposal, letting him finally cross Blackwater Bay.
edges.rationale: Velaryon sea power was the standing obstacle to Aegon's crossing; Corlys's submission removes it as a precondition — ENABLES, not CAUSES (Aegon still has to choose to sail).

edges.quote: but immediately set to pronouncing judgment upon the three “dayfly kings” who had ruled King's Landing during the Moon of Madness. The squire was the first to face his wroth, and was sentenced to
edges.note: Immediately upon returning and holding court, Aegon II passes judgment on the 'dayfly kings,' starting with Trystane.
edges.rationale: A specific judicial decision by the king (an actor exercising authority) — MOTIVATES the decision-event rather than an automatic CAUSES.

edges.quote: Aegon Il was determined to exact vengeance upon those who had betrayed and deposed him. He started with the crownlands, sending forth his own men and the stormlanders of Borros Baratheon against Rosby, Stokeworth, and Duskendale and the surrounding keeps and villages. Though the lords thus accosted, through their stewards and castellans, were quick to lower Rhaenyra’s quartered banner and raise Aegon’s golden dragon in its stead, each in turn was brought in chains to King’s Landing and forced to do obeisance before the king. Nor
edges.note: Restored to the throne and urged on by Alicent, Aegon II — 'not of a forgiving mind' — launches punitive raids on Rosby, Stokeworth, and Duskendale.
edges.rationale: An explicit, mother-urged decision by the king — MOTIVATES.

edges.quote: This campaign proved a grave mistake, for it only served to harden the hearts of the late queen’s men against the king. Reports soon reached King's Landing of warriors gathering in great numbers at Winterfell, Barrowton, and White Harbor. In the riverlands, the aged and bedridden Lord Grover Tully had finally died (of apoplexy from having his house fight against the rightful king at Second Tumbleton, Mushroom says), and his grandson Elmo, now at last the Lord of Riverrun, had called the lords of the Trident to war once more, lest he suffer the same fate as Lords Rosby, Stokeworth, and Darklyn. To him gathered Benjicot Blackwood of Raventree, already a seasoned warrior at three-and-ten; his fierce young aunt, Black Aly, with three hundred bows; Lady Sabitha Frey, the merciless and grasping Lady of the Twins; Lord Hugo Vance of Wayfarer's Rest; Lord Jorah Mallister of Seagard; Lord Roland Darry of Darry; aye, and even Humfrey Bracken, Lord of Stone
edges.note: The vengeance campaign hardens resistance across the realm, feeding the mobilization of the armies (Tully's riverlords foremost) that meet Borros at the Kingsroad.
edges.rationale: Real causation through intermediate steps (rebel mobilization, then march) rather than an immediate single-beat spark — CAUSES.

edges.quote: The turncloaks in his midst had put their plans in motion the moment they learned of Lord Baratheon’s defeat upon the kingsroad.
edges.note: News of Borros's defeat is the trigger the turncloaks (Perkin foremost) had been waiting for; Broome is thrown onto the Maegor's Holdfast spikes within the hour.
edges.rationale: Text states the coup plans were 'put in motion the moment they learned' of the defeat — immediate, same-hour consequence, TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: Tom Tangletongue was surprised in the castle yards as he was leaving the stables, and beheaded forthwith. “He died as he had lived, stammering,” says Mushroom. His father Tom Tanglebeard was absent from the castle, but they found him in a tavern on Eel Alley. When he protested that he was “just a simple fisherman,
edges.note: In the same hour as Broome's death, Tom Tangletongue is beheaded and Tom Tanglebeard drowned, part of the same coordinated coup.
edges.rationale: Parallel immediate consequence of the same news-of-defeat trigger (see H-E32) — TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: Queen Alicent was arrested on the serpentine steps as she made her way back to her chambers. Her captors wore the seahorse of House Velaryon upon their doublets, and though they slew the two men guarding her,
edges.note: Queen Alicent is seized on the serpentine steps by men wearing Velaryon colors, the same coup night as Broome's and the Two Toms' deaths.
edges.rationale: Same coordinated, immediate coup triggered by news of the kingsroad defeat — TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: The turncloaks in his midst had put their plans in motion the moment they learned of Lord Baratheon’s defeat upon the kingsroad.
edges.note: The kingsroad defeat is what sets Larys Strong's faction in motion the same night, culminating in the king's poisoning. [S204 adjudication: MOTIVATES->TRIGGERS — 'put their plans in motion', pre-existing conspiracy sparked]
edges.rationale: A human decision (Larys orchestrating the poisoning) mediates the military defeat and the king's death — agency-collapse check routes this via MOTIVATES rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: dead, long live the king,” Lord Corlys said, as he yielded up the city to their mercy.
edges.note: With the king dead, Corlys Velaryon and Prince Aegon ride out to meet the approaching riverlords under a peace banner and yield the city without a fight.
edges.rationale: Aegon's death is the precondition for a bloodless handover; the armies were converging regardless, so it enables the specific peaceful character of Cregan Stark's arrival rather than causing the Hour of the Wolf outright — ENABLES.

edges.quote: Even after his coronation, he would remain subject to a regent or regency council until his sixteenth nameday. Therefore, Lord Stark would have been well within his rights to pay no heed to the prince’s commands and proceed with the execution of Corlys Velaryon. He chose not to do so, a decision that has intrigued scholars ever since. Septon Eustace suggests that “the Mother moved him to mercy that night,” though Lord Cregan did not worship the Seven. Eustace further suggests that the northman was loath to provoke Alyn Velaryon, fearing his strength at sea, but this seems singularly at odds with all we know
edges.note: Aegon III's minority (age ten, uncrowned) means he is bound by custom to a regent or regency council the moment his kingship is settled during the Hour of the Wolf.
edges.rationale: Procedural/customary necessity with no real decisional gap — the boy's age alone forces the regency structure. TRIGGERS.

edges.quote: A lavish coronation was planned for the boy, to be followed by his wedding to the Princess Jaehaera. A cloud of ravens rose from the Red Keep, summoning the poisoned king's remaining loyalists in Oldtown, the Reach, Casterly Rock, and Storm's End to King’s Landing to do homage to their new monarch. Safe conducts were given, full pardons promised. The realm’s new rulers found themselves divided on the question of what to do with the Dowager Queen Alicent, but elsewise all seemed in
edges.note: Amid the 'False Dawn' that opens the Hour of the Wolf, the coronation and wedding to Jaehaera are already being planned as the mechanism to seal the peace.
edges.rationale: The political stabilization achieved across the Hour of the Wolf (Cregan's judgments settling rival claims) is the precondition that lets the already-planned wedding actually proceed — ENABLES, not CAUSES, since war could still have derailed it.

edges.quote: The death of a Kingsguard knight was greatly troubling to Ser Tyland, though Unwin Peake discounted Ser Damon Darry’s talk of sorcery and dragons and put down the death of Regis Groves and his men to outlaws.
edges.note: Regis's death at Harrenhal unsettles Hand Tyland Lannister, though Peake shrugs it off as ordinary outlawry.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition, character target): the killing troubles Tyland but produces no further organized action within this unit (the planned larger assault is preempted by the Winter Fever), so a character-disposition target is the honest edge rather than inventing a follow-on decision-event.

edges.quote: Lord Corlys lay in state beneath the Iron Throne for seven days. Afterward his remains were carried back to Driftmark aboard the Mermaid’s Kiss, captained by Marilda of Hull with her son Alyn.
edges.note: Corlys's death is immediately followed by the customary state viewing and funeral voyage to Driftmark.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: funeral rites are the mandated next beat after a great lord's death, not a contested decision.

edges.quote: The baseborn Alyn of Hull, now Alyn Velaryon, had been the Sea Snake's chosen heir, but his succession was not uncontested.
edges.note: Corlys's death opens the Driftmark succession that the disinherited 'silent five' cousins contest by force.
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: the death only creates the vacancy/dispute; the cousins' free decision to 'argue with swords' rather than accept the council's ruling is what actually produces the assassination attempt (agency-collapse check).

edges.quote: Lord Corlys's seat upon the council of regents had already been offered to an older and more seasoned man: Unwin Peake, Lord of Starpike, Lord of Dunstonbury, Lord of
edges.note: Corlys's death vacates the regency seat that the surviving regents offer to Unwin Peake — the opening rung of his rise to power. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: the death only creates the vacancy; the other regents' free choice to select Peake (over, e.g., the sixteen-year-old Alyn) is the actual appointing act.

edges.quote: Only three remained in King's Landing: Lord Peake, Lord Mooton, and Grand Maester Munkun, who came rushing back inside the Red Keep the moment Ser Robert Darklyn commanded that its gates be opened once again.
edges.note: Peake's prior seat on the council is what puts him among the handful of regents present in the city when Tyland dies, positioning him to seize the Handship.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: being a sitting regent is the precondition for Peake's presence and standing; his (and Munkun's) free decision to set aside the king's appointments and claim the combined office is the actual causing act.

edges.quote: In 133 AC, the new Hand decreed that it should be a day when those who had previously been judged would at last be punished for their crimes.
edges.note: Only once Peake has combined the Handship and Protectorship in himself does he have the authority to empty the dungeons and stage the mass punishment day.
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: holding the consolidated office makes the Feast possible, but decreeing it is Peake's own free decision to demonstrate his 'strength and rectitude.'

edges.quote: His leal subjects in the west had long been troubled by reavers from the Iron Islands, the young king declared, and who better to bring peace to the Sunset Sea than his new admiral?
edges.note: Fresh off his Stepstones victory and new admiralty, Alyn is immediately volunteered (through the king's mouth, Unwin's words) to sail against the ironmen.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targets the decision-event: Alyn's triumph and new office are what make him the obvious pretext, but sending him is Peake's own calculated decision (see I-E10).

edges.quote: The trap was neatly set.
edges.note: Gyldayn frames the ironmen mission as a deliberate trap by Peake — win or lose, it either breaks House Velaryon's power or rids the west of the Greyjoys, and either way removes the insolent Oakenfist from court.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Peake's enmity for Alyn drives the decision to dispatch him on a perilous mission; tier-2/disputed since the 'trap' characterization is the chronicler's interpretive judgment, not a stated fact on record.

edges.quote: If indeed Jaehaera Targaryen was murdered (and there is no shred of proof of that), it was surely done at the behest of the only truly plausible culprit: Unwin Peake, Lord Regent, Lord of Starpike, Lord of Dunstonbury, Lord of Whitegrove, Protector of the Realm, and Hand of the King.
edges.note: Gyldayn names Peake as the only plausible mastermind if Jaehaera's death was murder, though he stresses there is no proof.
edges.rationale: SUSPECTED_OF is capped tier-2 per the vocabulary; this is exactly that pattern — an actor implicated by motive and opportunity, never proven.

edges.quote: Were the king to sire an heir of his own body, that calamity might be averted...but before that could happen, Jaehaera had to be removed so Aegon could remarry.
edges.note: Gyldayn's stated logic is exactly the seed chain: Jaehaera removed -> king freed to remarry -> Peake's succession scheme (the Myrielle betrothal, seven days after the funeral).
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition, character target): tier-2/disputed because this reasoning sits inside the same hedged 'if murdered' passage as I-E11 — Gyldayn is laying out Peake's presumed motive, not asserting the causation as settled fact.

edges.quote: Some suggested that Lady Rhaena’s miscarriage might have made her so distraught she did not wish to live.
edges.note: One of several competing rumors for Jaehaera's despair: Rhaena's lost pregnancy is said to have driven the little queen to grief.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition, character target): explicitly one hedged rumor among several ('Some suggested... Others... countered'), so tier-2/disputed/unattributed rather than a firm claim.

edges.quote: in the order of succession...but Lady Rhaena’s pregnancy had ended in miscarriage, which left only the child growing inside Lady Baela on Driftmark.
edges.note: With Rhaena's pregnancy lost, only Baela's unborn child stands between the crown and 'the whelp of a wanton and a bastard' in Peake's eyes, sharpening his urgency over the succession.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition): the narrowed succession field is what Gyldayn cites as driving Peake's alarm and, by extension, his push to see the king remarried.

edges.quote: Unwin Peake had overreached himself. Thaddeus Rowan and Manfryd Mooton were outraged that he had not seen fit to consult them; matters of such import rightly belonged to the council of regents.
edges.note: Peake's unilateral Myrielle betrothal announcement provokes immediate, open protest from his fellow regents and great lords across the realm.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the backlash is the direct, undeliberated reaction to the betrothal announcement, narrated in the very next beat.

edges.quote: In the face of so much tumult, Lord Unwin was forced to think again. Though he remained determined to wed his daughter Myrielle to the king, he had to do so in a way that would not provoke the lords whose support he needed. Bowing to the inevitable, he mounted the Iron Throne and said, “For the good of his people, His Grace must take another wife
edges.note: The realm-wide backlash against the private Myrielle match is what produces Peake's public fallback: a Maiden's Day ball where the king ostensibly chooses for himself.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the text's own 'forced to think again... bowing to the inevitable' language marks this as real causation rather than a mere precondition (ENABLES) — the opposition leaves Peake no real alternative but to stage the ball.

edges.quote: wiser in the ways of power saw unseen hands at work and held their tongues. Were the Hand and his minions responsible for these tragedies and misfortunes, or were they happenstance? In the end it would not matter.
edges.note: A run of injuries and deaths among Myrielle's rival brides ahead of the ball is popularly blamed on Peake and his Fingers, though never proven.
edges.rationale: SUSPECTED_OF (capped tier-2): Gyldayn explicitly declines to resolve the question ('it would not matter'), so this is suspicion, not established causation — the disputed/suspicion register the assignment calls for.

edges.quote: “Brother,” Lady Rhaena said to Aegon, “if it please you,
edges.note: Rhaena (with Baela) rides into the throne room and presents Daenaera Velaryon to the king, breaking Peake's scheme. [S204 quote trimmed to single-line fragment; original spanned an OCR blank-line break]
edges.rationale: Role edge completing the ball hub with the marquee pivot moment the assignment calls out — 'the twins present Daenaera.'

edges.quote: As she stood before the king that Maiden’s Day, clad in pale white silk, Myrish lace, and pearls, her long hair shining in the torchlight and her cheeks flush with excitement, Daenaera was but six years old, yet so beautiful she took the breath away.
edges.note: Daenaera's presentation before the king is the ball's decisive moment.
edges.rationale: Role edge (ATTENDS) completing the ball hub for the chosen bride.

edges.quote: Caught in a snare of his own making, Lord Unwin Peake had no choice but to accept the king's decision with as much grace as he could muster.
edges.note: Aegon's surprise choice of the six-year-old Daenaera over Myrielle leaves Peake humiliated and enraged — 'the king's choice undoes Peake's scheme.'
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES (disposition): the ball's outcome doesn't force a single further act within this unit, but it curdles Peake's animus toward the king and the twins, the disposition that drives his later conduct.

edges.quote: The Hand declined quickly after that. The fever took his life in two days instead of the usual four.
edges.note: Tyland Lannister, already exhausted from tending the epidemic, succumbs to the Winter Fever himself.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: plain disease causation with no human decision mediating it.

edges.quote: Lord Westerling and Lord Mooton both grew ill. Lord Mooton’s fever broke and he survived, though much weakened. Roland Westerling, an older man, perished.
edges.note: Regent Roland Westerling dies of the Winter Fever, unlike Mooton, who recovers.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: plain disease causation.

edges.quote: The Dowager Queen Alicent of House Hightower, second wife of King Viserys | and mother to his sons, Aegon, Aemond, and Daeron, and his daughter Helaena, died on the same night as Lord Westerling, after confessing her sins to her septa.
edges.note: Alicent Hightower dies of the Winter Fever the same night as Lord Westerling.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: plain disease causation (verbatim source has an OCR-glitched 'Viserys |' for 'Viserys I', copied exactly per the quoting rule).

edges.quote: Widespread famine was reported in the North, and the Winter Fever descended on Barrowton,
edges.note: The ongoing epidemic finally reaches Barrowton, the first time it had traveled so far inland.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: straightforward continuation/spread of the same disease into a new location; no umbrella outbreak event node exists project-wide to route this through instead (concept.medical node is the correct anchor).

edges.quote: Meanwhile, the smallfolk of Fair Isle and such knights as still remained on the island rose up in red rebellion.
edges.note: Dalton Greyjoy's murder dissolves the ironborn fleet massed against him, opening the door for Fair Isle's smallfolk to rise.
edges.rationale: Text explicitly narrates the mechanism (line 93: the assembled fleet 'began to dissolve, as captain after captain slipped away for home' once word of Dalton's death spread) immediately before the rebellion. The uprising itself is a free collective decision by the smallfolk/remaining knights, not a forced mechanical consequence, so ENABLES (precondition removes the occupying threat) rather than CAUSES preserves their agency.

edges.quote: A sudden storm blew up as the fleet was making its way past the drylands west of Salt Shore, scattering the ships and sinking two.
edges.note: Wires the zero-edge stub: Alyn Velaryon commanded the fleet that lost two ships to this storm off Dorne.
edges.rationale: No causal antecedent or consequence is narrated for the storm itself (pure weather, no textual causal claim per hard rule 4), so a role edge is the honest wiring rather than forcing a causal type.

edges.quote: until the latter slew the former in a quarrel over Lord Farman’s daughter Lysa
edges.note: Gunthor Goodbrother's killing of Alester Wynch is the final beat of the Faircastle holdout inside the wider Fair Isle rebellion.
edges.rationale: Text places this explicitly as the resolution of the last ironborn holdout (Faircastle) within the same rebellion narrated at line 97 -- a beat inside a named event hub, not an independent causal chain.

edges.quote: Instead he whispered something in the admiral's ear, a secret that changed the course of Westerosi history.
edges.note: Drazenko Rogare's whispered secret at Sunspear is what sends Oakenfist to Lys to negotiate Viserys's ransom.
edges.rationale: A human decision (Oakenfist's choice to sail for Lys and then agree to Lysandro's terms) mediates the link, so MOTIVATES targeting the downstream decision-event (the ransom pact itself) is used instead of CAUSES, per the agency-collapse check.

edges.quote: The chance encounter between Alyn Velaryon and Drazenko Rogare at Sunspear had provided a perfect opportunity to effect the return of Prince Viserys to his brother
edges.note: Viserys's years-old captivity is the underlying condition that makes a ransom negotiation necessary at all.
edges.rationale: Marquee-spine check requested: links the 130 AC capture to the 134 AC ransom pact. Oakenfist's specific decision to travel to Lys and accept 'ruinous terms' is a free choice (explicitly editorialized by Mushroom as a mistake), so MOTIVATES on the decision-event, not CAUSES.

edges.quote: a dragon in the skies above the city
edges.note: Rhaena's first flight on Morning is one of the two 'momentous events' the text credits for the season's joy.
edges.rationale: The lysene-spring node's own Origins prose names Rhaena flying Morning as one of the two events the Seven Kingdoms rejoiced at during the Spring -- a beat inside that named event hub, not an independent chain.

edges.quote: Thereafter it was Aegon who was silent, for his old gloom had settled over him once again, and he seemed to lose all interest in his court and kingdom.
edges.note: Gaemon's poisoning-death drives Aegon III's relapse into gloom and withdrawal from court.
edges.rationale: Disposition change in a character, not a new discrete event -- the character-target form of MOTIVATES fits per the vocabulary definition.

edges.quote: On the morning of the twelfth day of the secret siege, Thaddeus Rowan was brought forth in chains to
edges.note: Marston Waters's regime tortures Rowan into a confession specifically to break the twelve-day standoff at Maegor's Holdfast.
edges.rationale: Marston/Graceford's decision to extract a confession is a deliberate human choice made under the pressure of the ongoing siege, not an automatic mechanical result -- MOTIVATES targeting the decision-event (per the preferred event-target form) rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: The traitors here are those who tortured him to bring forth this false confession.
edges.note: Rowan's coerced false confession, unraveled under Viserys's questioning, provokes the king's counterstroke: declaring Rowan innocent and ordering Graceford's arrest.
edges.rationale: The king's counterstroke is his own free decisive act in response to the exposed torture -- character-target MOTIVATES (per row 193's requested grounding) rather than collapsing his agency into CAUSES.

edges.quote: Yet as Ser Marston grasped it, Ser Mervyn seized his wrist, drew a dagger with his other hand, and plunged it into Waters's belly.
edges.note: Row 194: the exposure of the false confession leads Graceford to name Mervyn Flowers as a conspirator; Marston's attempt to arrest his sworn brother ends with Flowers stabbing him.
edges.rationale: A chain of individual decisions mediates this (Graceford naming names under threat, Marston choosing to confront Flowers personally, Flowers choosing to kill him) -- MOTIVATES on the downstream decision/killing-event rather than CAUSES, per the agency-collapse check.

edges.quote: Yet as Ser Marston grasped it, Ser Mervyn seized his wrist, drew a dagger with his other hand, and plunged it into Waters's belly.
edges.note: Role edge wiring the new death-of-marston-waters node: Mervyn Flowers is the killer.
edges.rationale: Standard AGENT_IN role edge for the newly minted event node.

edges.quote: Yet as Ser Marston grasped it, Ser Mervyn seized his wrist, drew a dagger with his other hand, and plunged it into Waters's belly.
edges.note: Role edge wiring the new death-of-marston-waters node: Marston Waters is the victim.
edges.rationale: Standard VICTIM_IN role edge for the newly minted event node.

edges.quote: King Aegon never warmed to him, it is true, but His Grace did not have a trusting nature, and the events of the past year had only served to deepen his suspicions.
edges.note: Row 204: the trauma and betrayal of the Rowan conspiracy year is what the text names as deepening Aegon's suspicion, which shapes his cold dismissal of the regents and Hand the instant he comes of age.
edges.rationale: Explicit textual grounding (not year-only adjacency, per hard rule 4) tying the conspiracy year to Aegon's mistrustful disposition toward regents/Hand; MOTIVATES on the specific downstream decision-event (the dismissal scene, newly minted since no node existed for this marquee beat distinct from the whole-period regency-of-aegon-iii container).

edges.quote: made an enemy of a man who had been amongst his most leal and devoted servants.
edges.note: Row 206: Aegon's curt dismissal turns Torrhen Manderly, a loyal servant, into an enemy who never forgives the king.
edges.rationale: Disposition change in a character (Manderly's turn against the king) -- character-target MOTIVATES.

edges.quote: “I shall,” King Aegon said. “You are sitting in my chair.”
edges.note: Role edge wiring the new aegon-iii-dismisses-the-regents-and-hand node: Aegon III is the agent of the dismissal.
edges.rationale: Standard AGENT_IN role edge for the newly minted event node; also grounds the row 204 seed's exact quoted line.

edges.quote: You have served me well, no doubt, as you served my mother before me, but it was my lords who chose you. You may return to White Harbor.
edges.note: Role edge wiring the new node: Torrhen Manderly is the Hand dismissed alongside the three lot-chosen regents.
edges.rationale: Standard role edge for the newly minted event node.

edges.quote: For the rest of that year, the Grand Maester served as both regent and Hand, for the realm required governance and Aegon had still not reached the age of manhood.
edges.note: Wires the zero-edge gathering-of-the-lords stub: Rowan's death leaves the regency vacant, and Munkun's solo caretaker rule leads directly into the summons that produces the 136 AC gathering.
edges.rationale: The vacancy is a precondition; the lords' answering of 'the king's summons' (a free choice by many independent third parties) actually produces the gathering, so ENABLES preserves that collective agency rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: Finally, in despair at reaching an agreement, Grand Maester Munkun proposed that three regents be chosen by lot.
edges.note: The assembled lords' failure to agree on new regents is what opens the door to Munkun's lot-selection proposal.
edges.rationale: Munkun's proposal (seconded by Viserys) is a specific free decision by named actors responding to the gathering's deadlock -- ENABLES, not CAUSES.

edges.quote: Instead the lords turned to the northman, Torrhen Manderly, Lord of White Harbor
edges.note: The same assembly that could not agree on regents also selects the new King's Hand.
edges.rationale: The lords' choice of Manderly (after rejecting Peake, Velaryon, Tully, and Blackwood as candidates) is their own free deliberation -- ENABLES.

edges.quote: The news of this monstrous dragonbirth and its bloody aftermath were greatly troubling to King Aegon, and soon led to angry words between His Grace and his brother.
edges.note: The monstrous hatchling that maims Laena directly and immediately sparks Aegon's ban on dragon eggs and the quarrel with Viserys. [S204 adjudication: TRIGGERS->CAUSES — Aegon's egg-ban decision mediates]
edges.rationale: Text states the second event followed 'soon' as a direct emotional reaction with no deliberation between -- an immediate spark, not a considered third-party decision, so TRIGGERS fits rather than ENABLES/MOTIVATES.

edges.quote: Lysaro was sentenced to be chained naked to a pillar before the Temple of Trade, where all those despoiled by him would be allowed to whip him
edges.note: Lysaro's capture and return to Lys makes his trial and eventual public scourging possible.
edges.rationale: Capture is a precondition; the specific brutal sentence is a free judicial decision by the Lys magisters (contrast his siblings' lighter, separately-deliberated punishments), so ENABLES rather than CAUSES.

edges.quote: Daeron conquered Dorne, but was unable to hold it, and died young.
edges.note: Row 7: fills a gap -- no role edge previously linked Daeron I to the Conquest of Dorne event node he is credited with.
edges.rationale: Honest, well-grounded role edge. See flags for why the fuller causal claim (failure to hold Dorne -> early death) is NOT typed here.

new_nodes.name: Maiming of Orys Baratheon and the ransomed lords
new_nodes.identity: In 7 AC, Orys Baratheon and the other lords ransomed back from Dornish captivity return to King's Landing with their sword hands lopped off by the Widow-lover, Wyl of Wyl, so they can never again bear arms against Dorne.

new_nodes.name: Aegon's reprisal on the Wyls
new_nodes.identity: In retaliation for the maiming of the ransomed captives, King Aegon personally descends on the mountain fastnesses of House Wyl with Balerion, reducing half a dozen of their keeps and watchtowers to molten stone; the Wyls survive by hiding in caves and tunnels.

new_nodes.name: Death of Meraxes
new_nodes.identity: During Aegon's renewed assault on the Hellholt, a Dornish defender's scorpion bolt catches the dragon Meraxes in the right eye; the dragon does not die at once but crashes to earth in mortal agony, destroying the castle's highest tower and part of its curtain wall.

new_nodes.name: Death of Rhaenys Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: Queen Rhaenys Targaryen perishes at the Hellholt in 10 AC when her dragon Meraxes is shot down; whether she lost her seat and fell, was crushed beneath the dragon, or survived only to be tortured to death by the Ullers is disputed by the sources, and her body was never recovered.

new_nodes.name: The Dragon's Wroth
new_nodes.identity: The two years following Rhaenys Targaryen's death, in which Aegon and Visenya burn every Dornish stronghold save Sunspear thrice over and place bounties on Dornish lords' heads, while the Dornish answer with assassinations and, in 12 AC, the Cafferen-Oakheart wedding massacre.

new_nodes.name: Nymor's letter to Aegon
new_nodes.identity: Princess Deria delivers a sealed letter from her father, Prince Nymor of Dorne, to King Aegon on the Iron Throne in open court; the letter's contents were never revealed, but reading it visibly shakes the king and leads him, after a night alone on Dragonstone, to accept Nymor's peace terms.

new_nodes.name: Aenys bequeaths Blackfyre to Maegor
new_nodes.identity: At his own coronation on Dragonstone in 37 AC, newly-crowned King Aenys I tells his half-brother Maegor 'we shall rule this realm together, you and I' and gifts him their father's Valyrian steel blade Blackfyre — a bequest Gyldayn explicitly calls unwise, since it fed Maegor's later claim that the throne belonged to whoever had the strength to seize it.

new_nodes.name: Death of Aenys I Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: Worn down by the Faith Militant's rebellion against his rule, King Aenys I collapses and dies at Dragonstone three days after learning that Poor Fellows have surrounded his son and daughter at Crakehall Castle, leaving the throne vacant and his half-brother Maegor free to claim it.

new_nodes.name: Maegor claims the Iron Throne
new_nodes.identity: Within hours of Aenys's death, Dowager Queen Visenya fetches Maegor from exile in Pentos; he lands on Dragonstone and has her crown him with Aegon the Conqueror's iron crown, bypassing Aenys's son and heir Aegon and beheading Grand Maester Gawen for objecting.

new_nodes.name: Death of Maegor I Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: With his support collapsed to a handful of crownland lords and his nephew Jaehaerys mustering an army at Storm's End, Maegor I Targaryen is found dead upon the Iron Throne at dawn, his arms slashed on the throne's blades and a blade through his neck; murder, suicide, and death-by-throne are all argued by the maesters, and he leaves no heir of his body.

new_nodes.name: Betrothal of Alysanne to Orryn Baratheon
new_nodes.identity: The regency council -- Alyssa, Rogar, and their advisors -- settles swiftly on Orryn Baratheon, Lord Rogar's youngest brother, as husband for the thirteen-year-old Princess Alysanne, deciding the match without consulting her or the king; word of the plan reaches Alysanne before any formal announcement and spurs her and Jaehaerys to flee to Dragonstone and marry in secret instead.

new_nodes.name: Jaehaerys pardons Lord Rogar
new_nodes.identity: Summoned before the Iron Throne, Rogar Baratheon kneels and submits himself to the king's judgment; Jaehaerys pardons his 'foolish words' on the condition that Rogar champion Queen Alysanne and honor Queen Alyssa, remarries his mother and former Hand to each other, and sentences Orryn to ten years' exile rather than death -- closing the regency's final crisis without bloodshed.

new_nodes.name: Rego Draz secures three foreign loans
new_nodes.identity: Jaehaerys's new, foreign-born master of coin, the Pentoshi merchant Rego Draz, plays the Iron Bank of Braavos against its Tyroshi and Myrish rivals to arrange three substantial loans, easing the Crown's empty treasury and mounting debt.

new_nodes.name: Dragonpit construction resumes
new_nodes.identity: With the Crown's debt eased by Rego Draz's three foreign loans, builders and stonemasons once again swarm over the Hill of Rhaenys to resume work on the half-finished Dragonpit.

new_nodes.name: Death of Prince Aegon (son of Jaehaerys I)
new_nodes.identity: Alysanne's son Aegon, named for the Conqueror and her lost brother, was born too early and died three days after birth; Alysanne forever blamed the Maidenpool attackers.

new_nodes.name: Great fire in the Disputed Lands
new_nodes.identity: A wind-driven wildfire raged for three days and nights across the Disputed Lands, engulfing half a dozen villages and trapping the free company the Adventurers between the flames and a Tyroshi host.

new_nodes.name: Famine of 59-60 AC
new_nodes.identity: Failed harvests across the North, riverlands, westerlands, Vale, and Reach following the cruel winter of 59-60 AC; larders and granaries emptied, some old folk went out into the snow to die so their kin might live, and hoarding drove up the price of bread across the Seven Kingdoms.

new_nodes.name: Storms scatter the Sun Chaser's fleet
new_nodes.identity: Three storms battered the Sun Chaser, Lady Meredith, and Autumn Moon far out in the Sunset Sea; lightning split the Autumn Moon, which went down with all hands, while the Sun Chaser was driven west onto three uncharted islands.

new_nodes.name: Death of Princess Daenerys
new_nodes.identity: Six-year-old Princess Daenerys, daughter of Jaehaerys I and Alysanne, dies of the Shivers a day and a half after waking her mother complaining of cold — the epidemic's most personally devastating loss for the royal couple, and the death that shatters their belief in the Doctrine of Exceptionalism.

new_nodes.name: Vaegon's insult of Daella in court
new_nodes.identity: Prince Vaegon publicly declares before half the court that he would never marry his sister Daella, mocking her illiteracy; the humiliation ends the presumed Vaegon-Daella match and forces the king and queen to seek him a different bride.

new_nodes.name: Collapse of Daella's courtship with Royce Blackwood
new_nodes.identity: A near-betrothal between Princess Daella and Royce Blackwood of Raventree falls apart when Daella learns the Blackwoods keep the old gods and she would have to say her wedding vows before a weirwood, which she refuses in horror ("I'd go to hell").

new_nodes.name: Death of Prince Valerion
new_nodes.identity: Prince Valerion, Jaehaerys and Alysanne's small and sickly son, dies a fortnight short of his first nameday, having never thrived since his difficult birth the year before.

new_nodes.name: Viserys names Rhaenyra heir
new_nodes.identity: Grieving the deaths of Queen Aemma and the infant Prince Baelon in childbed, King Viserys I breaks with the precedents of 92 and 101 AC and declares his daughter Rhaenyra his heir, naming her Princess of Dragonstone; hundreds of lords do obeisance and swear to defend her succession.

new_nodes.name: Aemond loses his eye at Driftmark
new_nodes.identity: At Ser Laenor Velaryon's funeral gathering at High Tide, ten-year-old Prince Aemond Targaryen secretly claims the dragon Vhagar; caught and mocked by Rhaenyra's sons as "the Strongs," he is blinded in one eye by Lucerys Velaryon's dagger in the ensuing brawl.

new_nodes.name: Death of Viserys I
new_nodes.identity: King Viserys I Targaryen dies in his sleep in the Red Keep on the third day of the third moon of 129 AC, ending the longest and most peaceful Targaryen reign and setting the Dance of the Dragons in motion.

new_nodes.name: Green council conceals the death of Viserys I
new_nodes.identity: Queen Alicent, Ser Otto Hightower, and the small council convene the night of Viserys's death, conceal his passing from Princess Rhaenyra and the realm, silence dissent (Lord Beesbury is killed), and resolve to crown Aegon before word can reach Dragonstone.

new_nodes.name: Coronation of Aegon II
new_nodes.identity: Ser Criston Cole crowns Alicent's eldest son Aegon II in the Dragonpit ten days after Viserys I's concealed death, the greens' pre-emptive answer to Rhaenyra's disregarded succession.

new_nodes.name: Murder of Prince Jaehaerys
new_nodes.identity: Acting on Prince Daemon's "an eye for an eye, a son for a son" vow of vengeance for Lucerys's death, the hired killers Blood and Cheese infiltrate the Red Keep and behead Aegon II's young heir, Prince Jaehaerys, before Queen Helaena, escalating the Dance into open atrocity.

new_nodes.name: The Sowing (the Red Sowing)
new_nodes.identity: Prince Jacaerys Velaryon's call for any commoner or dragonseed who could master one of Dragonstone's masterless dragons, following the deaths at Rook's Rest; also called the Red Sowing or the Sowing of the Seeds. Produced four new dragonriders — Hugh Hammer (Vermithor), Ulf White (Silverwing), Addam of Hull (Seasmoke), and Nettles (Sheepstealer) — at heavy cost in dead and maimed seekers.

new_nodes.name: Death of Maelor Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: King Aegon II's younger son Maelor, fleeing with Ser Rickard Thorne, is seized by a mob at Bitterbridge after Thorne is shot down on the bridge; chroniclers disagree on the manner of his death (crushed by Willow Pound-Stone per Mushroom, hacked apart by a butcher per Eustace, or torn limb from limb by the mob per Munkun).

new_nodes.name: Mysaria's Accusation Against Nettles
new_nodes.identity: Summoned by a suspicious Queen Rhaenyra, Mysaria tells the black council that Nettles has taken Prince Daemon to her bed and carries his bastard, tipping the queen's wrath into arrest and kill orders against the dragonseed riders and Lord Corlys.

new_nodes.name: Death of Helaena Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: Queen Helaena Targaryen, sister-wife of King Aegon II, throws herself from her window in Maegor's Holdfast to die impaled on the dry moat's iron spikes, at one-and-twenty, after half a year of captivity at Rhaenyra's court. The false rumor that Rhaenyra ordered her murder ignites the bloody riot of King's Landing that same night.

new_nodes.name: Accord of Storm's End
new_nodes.identity: In Lord Borros Baratheon's war camp outside King's Landing, Larys Strong and Queen Alicent strike a restoration pact with Borros, witnessed by Grand Maester Orwyle: pardons for Ser Perkin's men, Aegon II's marriage to Lady Cassandra Baratheon, and a betrothal for Larys, in exchange for stormlander muscle to retake the city and restore Aegon II to the Iron Throne.

new_nodes.name: Death of Regis Groves
new_nodes.identity: Ser Regis Groves of the Kingsguard, sent to reclaim Harrenhal from the self-styled 'witch queen' Alys Rivers, is killed before her gates within an instant of refusing to kneel to her infant son; chroniclers dispute whether by sorcery, crossbow, or sling.

new_nodes.name: Unwin Peake named to the council of regents
new_nodes.identity: Following Lord Corlys Velaryon's death, the surviving regents offer his vacated council seat to Unwin Peake of Starpike — previously excluded from the original seven regents and known to have been angered by the snub.

new_nodes.name: Death of Jaehaera Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: Queen Jaehaera, ten years old and the last surviving child of Aegon II, dies on the twenty-second day of the ninth moon of 133 AC after falling (or being thrown) from a window in Maegor's Holdfast onto the spikes of the dry moat below — officially ruled a suicide like her mother Helaena's death, but widely suspected as murder at Unwin Peake's instigation.

new_nodes.name: Opposition to the Myrielle betrothal
new_nodes.identity: Lords Rowan, Mooton, Tully, and Blackwood, Lady Arryn, Cregan Stark, and a wavering Grand Maester Munkun all protest Unwin Peake's unconsulted betrothal of his daughter Myrielle to the newly-widowed King Aegon III, forcing Peake to abandon a private match for a public Maiden's Day ball.

new_nodes.name: Maiden's Day curse
new_nodes.identity: A rash of injuries, scandals, and deaths befalling highborn maidens considered rivals to Myrielle Peake in the months before the Maiden's Day Ball; smallfolk spoke of a curse, while the shrewd suspected Unwin Peake's hand, though nothing was ever proven.

new_nodes.name: Death of Dalton Greyjoy
new_nodes.identity: Lord Dalton Greyjoy, the Red Kraken, is slain in his bed at Faircastle by his salt wife Tess, who cuts his throat with his own dagger and then drowns herself; his death instantly dissolves the ironborn fleet massed against Alyn Oakenfist and opens a succession crisis on the Iron Islands.

new_nodes.name: Death of Marston Waters
new_nodes.identity: Ser Marston Waters, Lord Commander of the Kingsguard and King's Hand, is fatally stabbed in the belly by his sworn brother Ser Mervyn Flowers when he goes to arrest him as a conspirator in the plot against King Aegon III; he dies of the wound that same night.

new_nodes.name: Aegon III dismisses the regents and Hand
new_nodes.identity: On his sixteenth nameday, King Aegon III enters the small council chamber, declares the regency at an end, dismisses lot-chosen regents Stackspear, Merryweather, and Grandison, and curtly sends Hand of the King Torrhen Manderly back to White Harbor, taking the governance of the Seven Kingdoms into his own hands.
